name: Abrams Samuel
biographical_note: This oddity is of an ancient and respectable Jew family, and with his brother acquired a large fortune in the tobacco business, being one of the oldest firms in that line in our city.

name: Abeel Jno. H.
biographical_note: A very old and rich house in the iron line, and if not now, were once worth double the sum.

name: Adams John
biographical_note: As the name imports, one of our numerous New England "immigrations." New York being in fact as much the metropolis of New England and the emporium of her commerce and of her enterprizing, adventurous and money-hunting population, as it is of the "Empire State" and of the American Continent. Our city has been none the worse, perhaps, for this influx from "down east," which, like the fresher, deposits always "en mouvement" upon their own sterile soil, and driven by ocean currents, (favorite element to your Yankee race) have quite overlaid the original and primitive aggregations of early Dutch settlers of this once city of New Amsterdam. Mr. John Adams flourished in the dry goods line, when great wealth was easily and rapidly acquired thereby.

name: Agnew Cornelius
biographical_note: An ancient New York family of great respectability, and a wealthy house.

name: Allen Stephen
biographical_note: Of a Long Island, New England, family, we believe, and for a long time kept one of the best sail lofts in the city; a man of austere temper, but reputed of stern honesty, strict probity, and great good sense, judg ment and energy, which, in despite of slight opportunities of education in early life, when he had "to rough it" with tarpaulin, elevated him to the Mayoralty and various other high and responsible trusts in the democratic party.

name: Alley Saul
biographical_note: Originally a chair or cabinet maker, and resided, we believe, at Charleston, S. C.; has since become in some sort a cabinet-maker in politics, in which some years back he took rather an ultra and prominent position. His fortune was acquired in the dry goods and importation and shipping business; being one of the numerous instances of an uneducated, unaided youth, among the mechanic class, who have, by their own indomitable energies and strong -intellect, advanced themselves to affluence.

name: Allaire James P.
biographical_note: Misfortune and temporary depression in the affairs of this enterprising citizen, whose foundry for steam machi nery and other castings are familiar by the excellent work turned out to all parts of the country, ought not to displace him from his true position, which, doubtless, his good sense and tact will soon enable him to resume, and to be what he is richly entitled to be, and what he once was, a demi-millionaire, to whom the mechanic classes in all the upper wards along the east river could point with conscious pride. His sister married the rich Mr. Haggerty. The Allaires, it would seem, are French, by the name.

name: Amory Jonathan
biographical_note: After a fiery ordeal, worse than that which his pipe-laying political associates are yet to pass through, this inerchant comes out unscathed from the savage assault, as it appeared to be, made upon his integrity by those who, when he was up in the world, flourishing as " aide de camp" of his Serene Highness, our small potatoe Governor Seward, were in ecstacies to have a smiling nod from him, to ruralize with him at their country villa, to press him to a seat in their carriage, or a spare queue in the "salon de billiards." Verbum sap. Mr. Amory's Appleton, cousins at Boston, will no doubt rejoice to have it in their power to aid in reinstating, to the extent of their power, this persecuted man to his former wealth.

name: Anderson Henry J.
biographical_note: The respected Professor of Mathematics in Columbia College, a self-made man, who, while yet a boy, gave such promise of scientific precocity, that the chair of mathematics in the venerable institution named, and which was his own alma mater, fell to him as by inheritance, before he had scarce attained manhood. His father, Elbert, was a fashionable and most capital cabinet-maker, and afterwards, as an army contractor in the war, exhibited proof of great energy and scope of mind, and these he bequeathed to his children as a richer legacy than the gold he had accumulated. Elbert Anderson was truly an honor to the mechanic class. One of his sons married into the ancient family of Ellery of Rhode Island, another, who was in one of the legations abroad, married Miss Phelps, daughter of Thaddeus P.

name: Andariese Barnet
biographical_note: A very excellent tailor and a very amiable man was Mr. Andariese. This worthy citizen was a pattern of propriety and industry for many a purse-proud aristocrat of the new-made gentry that turned their nose up at him and his calling.

name: Anderson Abel
biographical_note: Truly able, both in gold and in law knowledge. His father was an admirable shoemaker, and we believe honest man, who deserved all that wax and leather made for him.

name: Anthon John
biographical_note: His father was a highly respectable and learned physician, native of Germany. or son of a German. The sons of Dr. Anthon have nearly all distinguished themselves by their great ability. John, as a lawyer; Henry, as a divine, and Charles, the truly illustrious professor of ancient languages in Columbia Collage in this city. John added a large amount to his fortune by his marriage with

mean the management of the late New York and Erie Rail Road, under which he was crushed in his political aspirations, if he had any, but saved his bacon, we hope. It was a losing day when he got into the company of scurvy politicians and to be the bosom friend of William H. Seward.

name: Boyd James
biographical_note: A better sample of a rigidly honest and scrupulously exact mercantile man, and unobtrusive gentleman, no where exists, and we sincerely trust no commercial blast may ever seriously harm him in his honorable career.

name: Bradhurst Samuel
biographical_note: The Bradhursts are an old New York family, who have acquired their fortune in the drug line, to which "Alderman Sam" has appended a very comfortable bo- nus through his marriage with a daughter of the rich Thomas Pearsall, deceased.

name: Brandreth Benj'n
biographical_note: The celebreted vender of "Brandreth's Pills," whose virtues are said to consist in aloes, &c., compounded with the greatest care and selected of the finest qualities. Em- piric then though he be, he is not of the murderous tribe.

name: Brevoort H. Jr.
biographical_note: A worthy man and good citizen, but that he would ap- pear to be what he is not and strive to cast behind him the foreshadowing of by-gone events. To his kind hearted and venerable mother, whose weather-beaten features bore the brunt and pitiless peltings of many a piercing wintry northeaster of sleet and rain, as she sat vend- ing her vegetables, and carrots and York salad, under the unsheltered eaves of the market-house in the Bowery, is Henry indebted for the brilliant fortune and more brilliant education he received, all literally the fruits of her hard earnings. Further at present we know no- thing of Henry's lineage. He himself is distinctly and po- sitively of the order of "literateurs," though not "distingue" He is of the "novus homo," having, with his wife, a rich Southern widow, a very keen relish for all sorts of fashionable display, and like others of our would be no- billity a most abhorrent repugnance to any communica- tion with what such are pleased to call the "profanun vul- gus," and "basse classe," or "canaille."

name: Bronson Arthur
biographical_note: The Bronsons date from the father, Doctor B., of Con- necticut, the great financier, a marvellous quality in one of the faculty.

name: Brooks Sydney
biographical_note: Son of that John Jacob Astor of Boston Peter C. Brooks, brother of the late Governor B.

name: Brown James
biographical_note: English firm of great repute.

name: Bruce George
biographical_note: A worthy mechanic, who, in company with his bro- ther, from a printer, some years ago, became a type founder. By great industry and care, the possession of much natural shrewdness, and judicious operations in real estate, he has rendered himself wealthy, and is now mas- ter of a handsome fortune.

name: Bruen Matthias
biographical_note: When the great China merchant, Thos. H. Smith, of this city, failed, Bruen, through his son, Geo. W. B., who had married to Smith's daughter, became possessed of all Smith's ships, teas, &c., as Smith's bondsman. Govern- ment unwisely relinquished the greater part of what was due to the revenues, say $600,000, and this has made through rise of Smith's assets, houses, &c. a vast proper- ty for the Bruens, a New Jersey family, and originally dry goods merchants. Geo. W. lived in Italy and was in the Leghorn line.

name: Bryson David
biographical_note: An honest upright Irishman, one of the nabobs of the tanners and curriers of the "Swamp," with the Blood- goods, &c. Hasbeen an Alderman.

name: Buchanan Miss
biographical_note: Daughter of Thomas Buchanan, (deceased.)

name: Buckley Thomas
biographical_note: English Quaker, merchant, who made a good adven- ture, the first impulse to his fortune, when he married a daughter of the rich John Lawrence, deceased.

name: Buckley Henry
biographical_note: Son of Thos. (vide); and inherited a large estate by mar- riage with the daughter of Townsend Macoun, deceased, late Mayor of Troy.

name: Buloid Robert
biographical_note: A most worthy, upright merchant, and made his fortune by a retail grocery establishment in Broadway, celebrated for its choice assortments of rarest delicacies that can pamper the appetite of epicures, in delicious wines, li- queurs and confitures.

name: Bunker Wm. J.
biographical_note: Of the Nantucket Bunkers, of whom is the famous Commodore or "Admiral" Elihu S. Bunker, the first that drove a steamer on the wave of the ocean, having been the man who first navigated Long Island Sound in a steamboat-many, many years since.

name: Burnham (Estate of) Michael
biographical_note: A good man, and one that every printer should point to the memory of with a just pride for it was as much through his exactitude as the financier and practicai head and proprietor in the Evening Post as to William Cole- man's master talent with the plume, that such wealth ac- crued to both out of that ancient and able political jour- nal. Burnham was a man, too, of good instruction and pleasing manners and has left a proud name for his family.

name: Butler Benjamin F.
biographical_note: With native powers of mind, and most unpretending de- portment in the profession which he adorns, and respected every where for his sincere piety and pure life, Mr. Van Buren saw in him one whose sterling name would bring much capital to the democratic ranks. An early alliance of friendship, and the yielding temper of Mr. Butler, met with their full rewards in the very lücrative post to which his friend Mr. Van Buren finally assigned him as U. S. District Attorney for New York City. No one envies such a man's wealth, "Old Poins" and his "Report" to the contrary notwithstanding.

name: Campbell Jno.
biographical_note: Mr. Campbell, like his father, the late deceased and much respected proprietor of a large paper establishment, made the greater part of his fortun in that line, and has besides acquired much by inheritance. They are Scotch.

name: Campbell Geo. W.
biographical_note: Brother of John.

name: Carman Rich'd F,
biographical_note: A respectable New England name-carpenter by pro- fession, and by this gained his wealth; he himself wisely building his own houses for fools to buy or live in, the old adage being in this case reversed.

name: Carow Isaac
biographical_note: One of our most reputable New York merchants.

name: Cary Henry
biographical_note: Respectable, worthy merchants, he and his brother and trouble no one by any haughty display of their wealth-being gentlemen throughout, in mode and in manner.

name: Carnes Francis (Estate of his daughters)
biographical_note: A gentleman, every inch of him-and but few such specimens of a highly educated merchant. Resided a long while in Paris, where he was held in great consideration both among his countrymen and the elite of French society, for his fine taste, classical acquirements, polished manners, and ready wit. He is yankee born, of great respectability, and after many ups and downs of life and locating himself with his accomplished second wife at New York, his two beautiful daughters, just grown and by a former wife, have come into the above inheritance through death of a maternal great uncle.

name: Catlin Geo. (Estate of his father Lynde C.)
biographical_note: Time was, when in the healthy condition of banking institutions, to be a cashier of a bank like that venerable institution of which Lynde was the master spirit, was quite equivalent in consideration to a baronial title. Then, too exaggerated an importance was given in this money-making land to all who had the fingering of rag currency, while mere intellectual qualifications were in that "bank-note age" of Halleck, depreciated to below zero. Now the poverty and general depression of the times has drawn forth genius from its hiding-places and garrets, and given it a passable distinction, while every name almost that flourishes on the illuminated devices of a bank bill is looked at with scrutinizing suspicion. So wags the world as the equilibrium of those influences that govern society changes upon its axis. Of all these New England Catlins, George, the Indian Traveller and Painter, is indubitably the most distingue.

name: Cavanna Augustus
biographical_note: The only example, probably, of a hair dresser, and he an Italian, who, after steady application for many long years, has shaved his customers to the tune of some thirty thousand dollars, and invested it successfully in real estate so as to treble that sum. He has not lived in the atmosphere of Wall Street for nothing.

name: Cebra John Y.
biographical_note: The ex-alderman of the first ward, and an old and estimable citizen of New York, and though in years not advanced, may be deemed, from his useful public services, apart from his standing as a merchant, one of the fathers of the city.

name: Center Robert
biographical_note: Long a distinguished shipping merchant, of a numerous New York family of great respcctability.

name: Chabert I. X.
biographical_note: The French "Fire Eater," apothecary, &c., who by some "mandragora or other potent drug," burnt a hole in the heart of the rich old dowager, Mrs. Rapalye, of the ancient Dutch noblesse of Gotham-but the consuming passion removed the widow too soon for him from this earthly scene. The rent roll falling due, however, simultaneously with the arrival of the undertaker, yielded to the "devourer of moulten metal" only a modicum of the tons of silver and gold which he had hoped to have subjected to the fiery process of his crucibles. The black art of alchemy did not this time furnish him with the philosopher's stone or "elixir vitæ," and with the revulsion of the widow's large estate to its legitimate heirs, the salamander returned to his vocation of swallowing the coals of fire that her premature death had heaped upon his head.

name: Cheeseman Dr.
biographical_note: His father, an eminent quaker ship builder, gave the Dr. a good education, and by dint of Quaker influence in the government of the New York Hospital, got him there, and then through the same and a dashing horse and gig, into a rich quaker practice, for which the Doctor's personal elegance and bold ability as a cutter (surgeon) did much. If his celebrated uncle, Capt. Cheesman, who died so nobly under Montgomery at Quebec, was, as his comrades said; one of the finest looking men of his time, and took as much pains, as is is recorded, to make his toilette and beautify his person on the morning of that fatal day, as the Doctor does when visiting the saloons of the quaker noblesse, (who are ready to pay him $25 per visit) he must have been a second Beau Brummel. If the patronage of the quaker order of christians first brought note and wealth to the Doctor, his golden wings soon became fully fledged and strengthened, for he now doffed the shad bellied olive coat so velvety and neat, and the yellow breeches and white tops so polished, that had all in the matter of costume done him such faithful and essential service, and launched forth in to the gay world of frolick, fun and dance, of balls and banquets, and tableaux vivants. From being an exquisite of the first water in the quaker circle, he is now aspiring apparently to figure as the Henry Pelham of our new made nobility of the ultra fashionable society in the purlieus of upper Broadway. Ejection from communion with the quaker church, to which his wife, the daughter herself of a quaker preacher, was also subjected, widened the breach, and the Doctor is now virtually "hors de combat" for the little of the pure broad brim that will hurt him.

name: Chesebrough R.
biographical_note: A bighly respectable and worthy merchant, who acquired his property in the dry goods line.

name: Chesterman James
biographical_note: A worthy man and most capital tailor he was till an overgrown fortune honestly acquired in that unpretending vocation, obliged him to seek repose in a "hacienda" or "chateau," "a l'Anglois" in the environs of this imperial city.

name: Childs Samuel R. (Estate of his wife)
biographical_note: A clever, shrewd New England physician, and dipped too deep in politics and real estate, but has been saved from shipwreck by marriage with the fashionable milliner Mrs. Thompson, with whom he is now on a tour through Europe.

name: Clark Aaron
biographical_note: Of this state-long the valuable clerk of the legislative Assembly through all changes and revolutions of politics, which, however, one day at last landed him by one sudden and violent gyration of the wheel of fortune into a lottery office, where after multifarious dealing with the Sybelline leaves of fate, and the blanks and prizes of life's cheqner board, another equally sudden wrench of the wheel placed him in the marble house or capitol of the city, firmly seated in diplomatic black silk breeches and formidable wig, in the "fauteuil" of the mayoral-ty-dispensing justice and law, and cartmen's licenses and passengers permits, instead of lottery tickets and uncurrent money. "Viva la Bagatella! Leather and Prunella!" But whatever "gyrations" Aaron may have made, and to whatever changing points of the compass the vane of his friendship or hostility has turned, we rank him a notable example of the facility with which persevering active, sleepless industry and ambition, with native born shrewdness of mind, and pleasant social manners may, like the upheaving volcano, work their way to the surface of notoriety, through every superincumbent stratum of obstruction and adversity.

name: Clark Mrs. (widow of Benj'n)
biographical_note: Mr. Clark, from "down east," was famed as the Quaker lawyer, and friend of Daniel Webster; and a most worthy man and excellent jurist it is said he was.

name: Clason Augustus W.
biographical_note: Isaac Clason, first in the honorable vocation of a school master of a finished New England collegiate education, became one of the most prominent and enterprising shipping merchants of New York, and acquired and left a large estate. The lady of the present Postmaster, Colonel Jno. L. Graham is one of the daughters of Isaac Clason.

name: Clinton Mrs. H. (widow of Geo.)
biographical_note: Mrs. Hannah Clinton, daughter of Walter Franklin,

name: Clinton Charles A. (Estate of his wife)
biographical_note: This oldest son of the ever to be lamented and never to be forgotten Governor Dewitt Clinton, every way wor thy of that illustrious man, is happily placed beyond the reach of the pecuniary distress which that father he- roically succumbed to for the sake of enriching ungrateful millions with the benefits of those magnificent works of internal improvement which immortalize his name The estate of Mr. Charles A. Clinton comes through his mar- riage with a grand-daughter of Jno. Hone.

name: Cobb Oliver
biographical_note: A most excellent citizen and practical hard working man, beloved by all his brother merchants for his amiable disposition and plain republican manners and sterling virtues. Mr. Cobb has been long deemed one of the most respectable inhabitants of the first ward, where, though of whig politics, he has fulfilled the important trusts con- fided to him with equal satisfaction to all parties.

name: Coddington Jonathan I.
biographical_note: A merchant, and late the worthy Postmaster, a gen- tleman every inch of him-through life, and from the first jump of his parentage on this continent, two centu ries since, in the person of the famous Wm. Coddington, Esq., of England, first of Boston, then the founder and first Governor of Rhode Island, (1638.) He was the first merchant of New England, built the first brick house in Boston, and at his house at Newport was held the first Quaker meeting. Jonathan is a lineal descendant of Gov. C., and was long a distinguished merchant of our city, and like his great progenitor, an unswerving demo- crat.

name: Coe William S.
biographical_note: Of an old and respectable English stock, that were among the early patentees of Newtown and Jamaica, L. I., about two centuries past. For many years William S. Coe has been one of the hard working and most promi- nent and efficient gentlemen of the democratic party, and at last has reaped a comfortable indemnity for his services out of the various high trusts he has held, Surveyor, &c.

name: Coit Henry
biographical_note: Of New England, and by a steady, faithful attention to a safe mercantile business, has happily escaped the tornados that have overwhelmed the large operators, and comes out unscathed with a fortune of which every dol- lar counts two on the rag-standard estimate of former years of bloated paper bubbles.

name: Coit Henry A.
biographical_note: After roaming far and wide in commercial adventures, and passing through all the vicissitudes of clime and for- tune, this much esteemed and popular merchant finds him- self at last yet in the bloom of manhood, and with irrepres- sable energy of mind fixed in his native soil, and blessed here on his return with the luckiest, though the latest speculation he has made to wit, a charming yankee wife and rich heiress-the harrassing cares of the comp toir being thus converted into a "far dolce niente." He is the son of Levi Coit, long known as one of our most re- spectable merchants of an old New England family in the land of steady habits.

name: Coles Oscar
biographical_note: Of the Long Island branch of Coles, and inherits a large estate from his father, and another in expectancy through his beautiful wife, the daughter of a worthy citizen, Mr. George Brown, which latter, when mercantile reverses ruined him, had the noble energy of character to apply himself to the vocation of a hotel keeper, which, though perhaps less exalted, in common parlance, than the rank to which he was educated, proved infinitely more luera tive, and not only extricated him from the embarrassments that oppressed him, but yielded him splendid opulence. In this country, "Dieu merci!" such independence of spirit receives the approving reward of every virtuous mind whose good opinion is worth possessing, however swindling bankrupts, that have carried havoc in their pathway, may for a time brave public sentiment with their brazen effrontery, and dash jehu-like through the streets with their ill-gotten wealth, trampling under their horses' hoofs those even their betters, whom their villa- nous frauds have reduced to squalid beggary.

name: Coles Benj. U. (Estate of)
biographical_note: The Coles of Long Island and of New York, and pro- bably those of Virginia are descendants of Mr. Robert Coles and others of that name, (doubtless all brothers,) who settled at Boston, Lynn, &c. about two centuries since, and are among the most ancient and respectable of American names. The ancient earldom of Enniskillen in Ireland belongs to the family of Coles, but their Irish descendants who came over, brought, we opine, precious little of the moveables thereof with them.

name: Coleman (widow of William)
biographical_note: This estate was bravely acquired by her deceased hus- band, the celebrated Wm Coleman, former editor of the Evening Post. Through the most perilous storms of party strife, it must be conceded that this admirable man, even at the hazard of life and to the ultimate destruction of that life, gallantly maintained the old and impracticable doctrines of high-toned federalism in which he had been nurtured-but which, when war's alarms" brought us into collision with a foreign foe, he never pushed to that point of black-cockade ultraism which made him forget as some others of his party unfortunately did, the obligations of patriotism and devotion to his country.

name: Colgate William
biographical_note: A rich tallow chandler.

name: Collins E. K:
biographical_note: One of our most distinguished shipping merchants and owners of packet lines. A son of New England, and de- scended from a family that occupy an illustrious page in the annals of those heroic men who resolutely resisted puritan persecution.

name: Conklin Jonas
biographical_note: From Suffolk county, and a merchant.

name: Conover Stephen
biographical_note: In the war he was at his post, ready to defend our city from foreign invasion, and on the return of peace resumed his mercantile pursuits as a hardware merchant, and by a close and prudential attention thereto has met with the reward he so eminently deserved. He is a most worthy and unpretending citizen, and of an old New York Knick erbocker family.

name: Contoit John H.
biographical_note: Ice cream of the purest quality for near half a century may have melted away by the ton in the mouths of hun- dreds of generations who have frequented his well known fashionable garden. But Contoit's father, a busy old Frenchman, knew, it seems, how to solidify that perisha- ble luxury into heaps of massive and enduring gold, that will enrich all his posterity.

name: Corlies Joseph W.
biographical_note: A worthy, intelligent, honest, industrious, enterprising quaker merchant, who acquired his fortune in the auc- foneer business. The name is probably Huguenot.

name: Cornell Robt. C.
biographical_note: Of the ancient Cornell family, (originally Cornhill or Cornwall,) of Cornwall Hall, Cornbury, L. Island. Their progenitor escaped from the horrid massacre at Throg's Neck, 1643, in which the immortal Anne Hutchinson, the head of the colony, and most of the others perished.

name: Cornell Whitehead J
biographical_note: resides at Brooklyn

name: Cornell Peter C
biographical_note: resides at Brooklyn

name: Corse Israel
biographical_note: Quaker, leather merchant, deceased, descendant, how ever, of a man of much military note, Colonel Israel Corse, of the American revolution-native, we believe, of Long Island.

name: Coster John G.
biographical_note: And his brother, Henry A. deceased, of a respectable family in Amsterdam, and began here as merchants soon after the revolution, and by honest industry amassed a great fortune. While Napoleon held Holland, they, through confidential correspondence were enabled to know how far to push the trade thither, and thus in re turn importations of Gin found so rich a harvest that their wealth rapidly accumulated from that hour. They were gentlemen born, and of irreproachable integrity.- The three millions fortune the two brothers amassed is nearly all melted away before it has hardly got into the hands of their children. Dr. Hosack, deceased, made a deep gouge into that of Henry's widow, but where is it?

name: Cottinet Francis
biographical_note: Now one of the oldest and always has been one of the most respectable and prominent of our French importers of silks, &c. He married the accomplished daughter of General Edward Laight, being one of the few instances of the alliance of respectable French and American fami- lies.

name: Cozzens W B.
biographical_note: One of the most ancient and honored names in the early annals of New York, when it became an English province.

name: Cram Jacob
biographical_note: It is well this worthy citizen had accumulated his large fortune by the worm of the distillery before Tom Marshall, that maw-worm from Kentucky came on the scene to get up by his spirit-stirring and imspiring eloquence a tee totaling crusade against the consumers of every denomina- tion of ardent liquor. For it is ten to one in another 20 years it will be held as a capital crime to taste even a drop of Chateau Margot wine. That promising young comedian, Mason, the nephew of the immortal Kemble, made a good day's work o it when his fine personation of master Clifford obtained for him the hand of the fair daugh- ter of this almost demi-millionaire.

name: Crosby Wm. B.
biographical_note: As the great nephew of the rich Col. Henry Rutgers, of the ancient Rutgers family of this ciiy, he inherited an immense estate. His wife, through her mother, is grand daughter of Gen. Wm. Floyd, one of the signers of the Declaration of Independence.

name: Cruger Mrs. Douglas
biographical_note: Her father was Geo. Douglass, a Sco'ch merchant, who hoarded closely. His wine cellar was more extensive than his library. When George used to see people specu- lating and idle, it distressed him. He would say "Peo ple get too many idees in their heads. Why don't they work?" What a blessing he is not alive in this moon- shine age of dreamy schemings. The Crugers were of Bristol, Eng., of which the one that first came out here, "Old Harry," father-in-law of the present judge Thos. Oakley, of the Superior Court, had been Mayor.

name: Cutting F. R.
biographical_note: The Rev. Mr. Cutting, his grandfather, was the princi pal of a famous grammar school at Hempstead befor the American revolution, and from him the illustrious Dr. Sa muel L. Mitchill received his first lessons, as did also ma- ny of the sons of the gentlemen of Long Island of those times. His son William married a Livingston, and by this aud that hourly source of accumulating wealth, the Ful- ton steam ferry boats established by him-they have be- come extremely rich.

name: Dash John B.
biographical_note: Respectable hardware merchant.

name: Dater Philip
biographical_note: Dutch, of New Jersey, and probably son or nephew of Abraham D., an iron master, who owned a considerable forge on the Ramapo, in Rockland county, N. Y.

name: Davis Chas. A.
biographical_note: Originally, on his father's side, it is said, of a Portuguese family, through a Portuguese lady saved miraculously from the earthquake of Lisbon to become the betrothed wife of the grandfather of Charles A., who was then British Consul at that capital. So far for Major Jack Downing No. 2, who did not altogether play fair to Zeba Smith, the real Simon Pure Jack, whose plumes he bor- rowed and made to fit with such counterfeit resemblance to those of the original owner.

name: Dawson William
biographical_note: Is an English gentleman in the broker line and if not worth this sum himself, will inherit it through his wife, the daughter of Peter A. Jay.

name: De Forest Benjamin
biographical_note: A most successful shipping merchant in the West In- dia trade.

name: Dekay George
biographical_note: Of an ancient New York family, and while yet a youth was in high command as a commodore in the naval ser- vice of Buenos Ayres, where he gained laurels and libe- ral pay and prize money by his courage and nautical skill. On his return home he married the only daughter and child of the lamented Dr. Drake, the poet. The wife of Dr. Drake was the daughter of Mr. Eckford. Ano ther daughter of Mr. E. married Dr. James E. Dekay, brother of the commodore.

name: Delafield Dr. Edward
biographical_note: Has re-estab'ished, by patient industry and sleepless perseverance, more than by any brilliant talen's he pos- sesss in his profession, the fallen fortunes of his house, a great name some forty years since, before his father's bankruptcy. An eye infirmary, with the usual quantom sufficit of puffing that heralds these very charitable insti- tutions, and which they now, enri hed by such "applt- ances," would call chalatanerie, paved the way for that patronage which the aris ocracy have since bestowed upon him Now, firm in his saddle, he says, Viva la Humbug! Let those laugh who win for he has doubied his fortune by marrying he grand-daughter of the late Gen. Wm. Floyd. His brotar John has been an im- mense op rator in stocks, and Major Joseph, another bro ther, is a man of much scie tific acquirements.

name: Dalaplaine John F.
biographical_note: His father was a rich old New Yorker, and John F. married also a daughter of the rich Isaac Clason.

name: Delmonico John
biographical_note: Who has not heard of the French-Italian Restaurant of Delmonico and his brother Peter. No parallel case of an Italian reaping such a fortune has ever occurred in the history of this city. But if the best of fare and choice wines and unceasing politeness to their guests merit for- tune, they eminently deserve what they have earned. The Delmonicos are natives of that part of Switzerland which borders on Italy. They may be said to have first in- troduced into our city a taste for those fashionable com- forts which can no where else be found but in the cuisine of a French Restaurateur, and since then others have

tried the experiment, but no one with the complete suc- cess which has attended their more perfect arrangements. Tact, ditpatch, cleanliness, courteous attention, and the choicest dishes and liquors served at a reasonable charge with pre-eminent skill are the elements of their populari- ty. John, besides the splendid hotel he has built on the French plan in the very business heart of the city, owns also a large country seat at Newtown, L. I. They are now among our most monied men, cash in hand, and withal of great prudence. The endorsement "Delmoni- co" would go as far as almost any name.

name: Deluze L. P.
biographical_note: Foreigner-dry goods-respectable.

name: Demilt Samuel
biographical_note: Old New Yorkers, and have made clocks and watches to some purpose. Their chronometers don't go upon tick.

name: Depeyster J. W. (Estate of John Watts)
biographical_note: One of our oldest Dutch families, that like good old wine or cheese need no eulogy. Look at the list of Bur- gomasters.

name: De Rham Н. С.
biographical_note: A foreign house, intermarried with our Moores.

name: Desbrosses (Estate of James)
biographical_note: This is one of the old New York estates and families, which have accumulated like many others from large real possessions, from long before the American revolu- tion, and passed by intermarriage into other names The Desbrosses family, long deemed by the false standard in this country, for being m possession of considerable real estate for half a century or upwards, as among the "pa- tres conscripti, are any thing but patrician in their ex- traction. Old Elias Desbrosses and his brother, (the latter grandfather of James above,) came out to this country as among the very poorest of the Houguenot emigrations, near two centuries since. They settled here, and carried on their vocation as French confectioners in a very hum- ble way, very probably the fashionable caterers for the few fetes and festivals that took place among the elite of society in those primitive days. Their two maiden sis ters being at the same time the most esteemed milliners of that day, and furnishing the latest Parisian fashions, to our then belles of Broadway, exciting thus at that ear- ly period a taste for French costumes which is now all the rage, and not extraordinary when we reflect how well the classical taste of the French in matters of fe- male attire is adapted to the beautiful and sylph-like forms of American ladies. Through the aid of contec- tionery and millinery the grandson, James Desbrosses, above, became exceedingly wealthy and left his property to two daughters, one of whom married John Hunter, at present a senator in our legislature, and the other a Mr. Overing, of Rhode Island. The fate of the Chadeayne (sometimes spelt anciently and improperly Shadden, as pronounced.) family, is singularly contrasted with that of the Desbrosses. The Chadeaynes were also Huguenots, and came hither at the same time with the Desbrosses.- In their own country of France the Chadeaynes (asthe Desbrosses them-elves have often admitted,) were of the ancient aristocracy, into whose society the Desbrosses could not have obtained admission. Here the picture, under the operation of the rotatory topsy-turvy machine- ry of our wholesome system of polity, has been sadly reversed, and the Chadeaynes have been for generations down among the "hewers of wood and drawers of wa- ter," and "none so poor to speak to them."

name: Dey Anthony
biographical_note: But few of our eminent lawyers have struck out into such successful speculations in real estate as this gentle- man who, however, possesses far more shrewdness and long-reaching sagacity than most men, either in or out of the legal or any other profession. His operations in latter years seem to have been much confined to the management of his property.

name: Dickinson C.
biographical_note: Of an ancient, honored house in the early annals, and this one a ship chandler and shipping merchant, who thus acquired, near half a century since, a splendid fortune. The late Edgar Evertsen, Esq, son of Nicholas, deceased, married a daughter of Mr. Dickinson.

name: Donaldson Robert
biographical_note: These rich Donaldsons are Scotch gentlemen.

name: Douglas Wm.
biographical_note: Geo. and William, sons of Geo. above. See Cruger.

name: Drake James I.
biographical_note: The Drakes are an old New York family. This one's father was in the clothing line, and well did he feather his own nest. The other rich Drakes were uncles of James J. D., and all hail from Admiral Sir Francis D.

name: Drake Mrs. Susannah
biographical_note: Her husband was brother of James Drake, deceased. On of her daughters married John R. Townsend (vide.)

name: Draper Simeon, Jr.
biographical_note: He and his 8 or 9 brothers, remarkable the most of them for their fine personal appearance and intelligence and business habits, inherited from their worthy father, of New England, came to New York to seek their fortune upon this wide field of mercantile enterprise, and well have they for the most past succeeded. Simeon Jr. but for his unfortunate connection with party politics would doubtless still possess the fortune, and wealth which his active industry acquired, but at all events may count with certainty on that which we have given above as the probable portion on the lowest estimate of what will ac- crue to his wife from her father, the wealthy John Hag- gerty, Esq.

name: Drew Daniel
biographical_note: Of the firm of Drew, Robinson & Co., brokers, and owner of the steamboat Rochester, a shrewd, money making man.

name: Dubois Cornelius
biographical_note: One of our oldest, wealthiest, and most respected of merchants of French extract of great distinction.

name: Edgar Wm.
biographical_note: Son of William, who began as a poor Irish emigrant, and thus gradatim and seriatim worked his way up the ladder to become the holder of a great estate. Even the "Le Roy" blood did not think an alliance with self- made Mr. Edgar disparaging-nor was it.

name: Embury Peter
biographical_note: A worthy grocer and old "cit," father-in-law of the lovely poetess and writer, Mrs. E.

name: Evertsen (Estate of) Nicholas
biographical_note: Of the very oldest, purest Dutch blood, was this truly eminent counsellor and most estimable gentleman. The progenitor of the family was Commodore Evertsen, the "Admirai Van Tromp" of those days in the bloodless exp-di ions of cock boats which the Dutch Governor sent cruising down the Long Islard coast to keep those Eng- lisa "vassals" in due subjection.

name: Faile Edw. G.
biographical_note: Rich grocers, he and his brother, and muagre thenam would be the last to fail in whatever obligationsbecom them as honorable men.

name: Fanshaw D.
biographical_note: Another of our worthy types-self-made.

name: Ferris Chas. G.
biographical_note: Our worthy Congressman-inherited a large fortune from his father, who made it while in a public situation, as inspector of flour, we believe. A very ancient family of this province.

name: Field H. W.
biographical_note: Of an ancient New England family of great respectability were the Fields. "Mr. Robert Field" being one of the earliest settlers at Boston.

name: Field Benjn H.
biographical_note: Of an ancient New England family of great respectability were the Fields. "Mr. Robert Field" being one of the earliest settlers at Boston.

name: (Field Heirs of Moses)
biographical_note: Of an ancient New England family of great respectability were the Fields. "Mr. Robert Field" being one of the earliest settlers at Boston.

name: Fish Preserved
biographical_note: Of an ancient family of England, who located over two centuries since, first at Saugus, near Boston, then at Sandwich, and finally at Newtown, Long Island. So the merry tale of Nantucket that a foundling on the beach rescued by a whale boat from a watery grave was thus named, falls to the ground.

name: Fitch Asa Jr.
biographical_note: Of New England-long a merchant at Marseilles, and through many ups and downs of life, and now in the vale of years, has come back with a constitution enfee. bled, but bringing such wealth that he ranks as one of our richest and most recherche old bachelors. It is not every "lame duck"-a common phrase of his-that could come out so tall, and loom so large and lofty in haute societe, after passing through the severe ordeal Asa has submit- ted to. But paciencia! as the Spaniard says.

name: Foster Andrew
biographical_note: Scotch. The other Fosters here are of an emigrant fa- mily from New England that early settled at Jamaica, L. I.

name: Foulk Joseph
biographical_note: The Foulks are wealthy English gentlemen who have been for many years in the very first ranks of our high- minded honorable merchants.

name: Fowler Theo.
biographical_note: The father deceased about the close of the revolution- ary war, became suddenly wealthy. We believe he was an officer under Washington.

name: Fox George
biographical_note: We cannot trace his lineage to Geo. Fox, the shoema- ker, founder of the Quakers.

name: Friedler Ernest
biographical_note: A German merchant.

name: Furman Gabriel
biographical_note: A very respectable and ancient English family, one of the earliest among those who colonized Long Island.- Formerly Superintendent of the Alms House. Judge Jno. T. Irving, deceased, (brother to Washington Irving) be- came enriched by marrage with a daughter of Gabriel Furman.

name: Gallatin Albert
biographical_note: Swiss. Formerly Secretary of the Treasury of the U. States.

name: Gardiner David
biographical_note: To the ancestors of this distinguished family belonged Gardiner's Island, Suffolk, Co., L. I. One was called "Lord Gardner," by some of his poor tenantry

name: Garner Jas. G. (brother of the preceding)
biographical_note: It is a grateful task to note in conspicuous terms, the fortunes of these two distinguished merchants, who have literally won laurels and golden opinions. Failing in 1832, they rose by great energy and industry to wealth, and in 1835 paid off every shilling of their obligations. with interest to boot. Of how few can this be said? To most people, therefore, who are now revelling on the ruined fortunes of the widow and the orphan, the remin- iscences of the past are harrowing and scorching, if their consciences are not seared.

name: Gebhard F. (Estate of)
biographical_note: An old German, now dead; his large estate the result of years of patient industry.

name: Gelston (Estate of) David
biographical_note: Old Mr. David Gelston, (deceased) was a child of for- tune in the matter of official emoluments, having held the collectorship of this port under sundry democratic admin- istrations, and thus, though beginning poor in life and of obscure family in Suffolk Co., made any amount of money, whereby his family are placed far out of the reach of want.

name: Gifford Arthur N.
biographical_note: Mr. Gifford was educated and graduated as a physician, but not choosing as a gentleman to wade through the tortuous and muddy paths by which some of the members of an overpopulated and starved profession are compelled to get their bread, and being too high-minded to resort to low/arts and cunning to obtain distinction, made his debut in the broker line in Wall street, and has there operated to an extent to justify his most sanguine expectations.- He glories still in the beatitude of single blessedness.

name: Gilbert Mrs. (widow of W. W. G.)
biographical_note: W. W. Gilbert, deceased for many years one of the most eminent of cur fashionable retail dry good mer- chants in Broadway, and uncle of the present "Garry G." the famous minstrel politician. "Garry" and William W. were the Stewarts of that day among the lady pur- chasers.

name: Gilley Mrs
biographical_note: Her husband, deceased, by birth Scotch, acquired his fortune in the book ine, a the principal partner in one of the most cei bra ed stores in that business of with our ti y could then boast.

name: Glover (Estate of) John J.
biographical_note: Long a highly respected merchant and one of the earliest of the resident English dry good importers. His numerous daughters all save one or two are married and enjoy the patrimony of their father's honest earnings. One married Mr. Holley of Connecticut, another Alfred P. Edwards, Esq., a third the Rev. Mr. Eastburn, and a fourth the Hon. C. C. Cambreling.

name: Goelet George
biographical_note: Son of Peter P. G., deceased.

name: Goelet Peter (Another son of do.)
biographical_note: Peter P. and Robert Ratsey G., sons of an old and re- spectable merchant, and both married daughters of Thos. Buchanan, Scotch, merchant of this city, prior to the American revolution. Thos. Buchanan's father was Mayor of Glasgow, in Scotland.

name: Gomez A. L. (in right of wife,)
Goodhue Jonathan
biographical_note: Universally beloved for his good heartedness, which, however, is often imposed upon by artful dupes, particu- larly under the mask of religion. A first rate merchant most humane and charitable to the poor. His father was an honest blacksmith of Salem or Portland, down east, and the rumor is that there is in Mr. Goodhue a
touch of the aboriginal or Indian blood. His features, expression and complexion favor this impression.

name: Graham Bernard
biographical_note: A self made man. Was an Irish porter and laborer to Peter Harmony, (see below) and from thence arose to be his partner.

name: Greenwood (Estate of) John
biographical_note: This it may be said was the father of New York Dentists. Of a highly respectable family in the Bay State, and cradled in the revolution his youthful ardor while but a boy impelled him into the ranks of Washington's army, and after the close of the war, in successful practice in this city as a dentist he rose to great renown, and was the dentist of his former great captain, (Washington) up to his death. He was a venerable man of great originality and shrewdness of mind on all subjects, a great reader and deep thinker, generous and chivalrous in disposition, of ready wit and full of the anecdote and lore of the past. In his profession his expert and adroit workmanship, bold ingenuity and resources under all difficulties, acquired him a reputation that left him without a competitor, and brought him large gains, which fortunately invested, accumulated to a great estate for his worthy children, two of whom, Isaac and Clark, though thus inheritors of wealth, felt an honorable pride in following their father's profession, and also arrived to great eminence.

name: Griffin George
biographical_note: Literally a very tall and most accomplished lawyer, whose fame at our bar for near half a century is too well established and known to require comment. We believe he is of a New England family.

name: Grinnell M. H.
biographical_note: The popular ex Congressman, born in New Bedford, enterprising and liberal; he realizes our beau ideal of a New York merchant.

name: Griswold George
biographical_note: One of the household of the "Patres Conscripti," of Connecticut. There is no parvenuism here. He is now ranking in the line of India merchants in more senses than one.

name: Grosvenor Seth
biographical_note: Dry Good Merchant, of an old New England family, and brother of the once distinguished federal orator and Congressman, deceased. (Thomas P. G.) Seth is uncle of the widow of that late brilliant meteor in judicial acumen, S. A. Talcott, that set so prematurely in clouds and darkness.

name: Green John C. (India merchant)
biographical_note: Married the daughter of Geo. Griswold, (above)

name: Hadden David
biographical_note: Scotch Dry Good Merchant, respectable.

name: Haggerty John
biographical_note: (See Austin, David.) A foremost man among our merchants, and has acquired great wealth as an auctioneer.

name: Haight Richard K.
biographical_note: Of an old New York family. He and brothers are sons of an eminent saddler. Richard, like the Muscovite Emperor, worked as a day laborer in France to acquire a certam mystery in trade touching prints for hat linings, and now reaps the monopoly obtained by his spirited undertakings, together with a fortune inherited. Madame H. is the fair authoress of the entertaining book of Travels to Egypt, &c, and is a fine sample of a pretty Long Island girl from old Suffolk.

name: Hale David
biographical_note: This is the "Vicar of Bray," editor of the Journal of Commerce, a yankee by birth, but a Jesuit at heart. and as true a disciple of Ignatius Loyola as though he had been educated at the Sorbonne. As an editor unsparing, vindictive, filled with sanctity and duplicity to the crown of his head--being all things to all meu, both in religion and politics, and hiring out his Tabernacle to whoever pays the best.

name: Hamilton J. C.
biographical_note: Son of the renowned statesman Hamilton, and also his biographer. Married a daughter of the rich Dutch merchant Vandenheuvel, deceased, and owns thereby the American Hotel.

name: Hammersley Lewis C.
biographical_note: Of an old and respectable New York family. His father, Thomas, acquired a large fortune in the dry good line.

name: Harper Brothers
biographical_note: These are the four remarkable men, who of a highly respectable English family that anciently settled upon Long Island, have risen by their enterprise and industry from journeymen printers to become the most celebrated publishers in America, and among the names that will go down to posterity with the Galignanis and Murrays, and Constables of Europe. The Harpers have done more practically for the encouragement not only of domestic authors but for the diffusion of the literature and science of other countries than all the universities, colleges, academies and schools of the Union put together. Nor have their profits, though large, been proportionate to their truly patriotic and noble exertions. New York may be proud of the birth-right of these men, and that she has produced these great operators, these high-priests of Minerva, who have erected here a holy temple, as it were, in the midst of money-changers and shopkeepers, to keep alive the undying fires of intellect, to guard and trim the lamp where genius may kindle its illuminating torch and learning diffuse abroad its milder beams. Without them or some such as shall follow in their footsteps, darkness would come over the land. But for these humble printers who have appreciated the spirit of the times, and saw and felt the necessity of acting in keeping with the accelerated movement of thought and of industry which steam-power has given to this age, our people in the dissemination of that most useful and potent of all influences, mental power and the knowledge which it produces, would have been a century behind the rapid and advanced progress they have made. It was to compete with them that the free press of our country was stimulated to endeavors and prodigious results, which have astonished the world. The lightning-like rapidity with which the choicest coinings of the brain in foreign lands, are almost immediately made the common properperty and universally circulating cheap commodity of our own people, threaten in time to make the book-business, if not periodical literature also, a dead letter and destructivé pursuit to those who embark in it. For it would seem that every production that hereafter emanates from the closet of the author must seek this every-day channel of the penny press, to reach the public market. To the universal taste for reading whatever is worthy to be perused, and which the Harper cheap press gave the first great impulse to, are we then to ascribe indirectly the immense diffusion which is now immediately given by the penny pressin our country, to whatever reaches us from abroad, fresh almost and at the moment that it is wrought from the mind of its author. Once thus gratified with what, from the extreme cheapness of penny publications may be deemed the almost gartuitous offerings to our countrymen, of the cream and marrow of foreign literature, it is not to be imagined after the practical workings of this operation as exemplified recently in the national reception of Boz, and the familiarity of every one that

Can read, with all the writings of such shining luminaries as he and Bulwer, and others, that our people will ever surrender so dear a privilege, however wrong it may be fact, when the subject of international copy-right is fairly Canvassed. The Harpers, be it added to this peroration, are exemplars also in private life and as moral and religious men, being all of them, we believe, members of the Methodist Church and of many Temperance and charitable societies. So much for a brotherhood of New York born printers, to show what mechanic industry can accomplish.

name: Harmony Peter
biographical_note: A Spanish cabin boy was his beginning, and he after wards became familiar with the West India seas. We are in the dark as to his career afterwards, except that he is now one of our most opulent merchants.

name: Hart Eli
biographical_note: The Flour Merchant, whose store was sacked by rioters.

name: Hatch George W.
biographical_note: Rawdon, Wright and Hatch are the celebrated bank engravers, and another sample of intelligent, spirited mechanics, making for themselves a name and a fortune incomparably more to be prized than the wealth which has Leen acquired too often by mere mercantile gambling. There is substance, and truth, and reality-something tangible, and definitive, and susceptible of ocular demonstration and utility in the fruits of mechanic labors, but what visible means of livelihood, and what direct and practicle utility do we see in many other professions, to wit: swindling stock operations, but which are yet deemed more reputable than the walks of mechanic life. The time is gone by, however, when dreaming speculators and fancy operators can any longer sneer superciliously at the "brawny arms" and "russet palms" of the honest laborer. Thus much has been done by breaking up a false system of credit, and by consequence, breaking up the nests of lounging, idle upstarts, that like mushrooms on a dung-hill, sprouted up out of the corrupt masses of rag-paper and spurious capital. May Rawdon and Hateh never lend their burin and mezzotinto to any paper that does not command yellow mint drops instanter at the counter on the very face of it. Mr. Hatch is half brother of Ex-Governor Throop, and to judge by his early prosperous career cannot be said to have counted his chickens before they were hatched. Mr. Rawdon is a branch of the Irish family of Rawdon, Earl of Moira, who was so distinguished as a British partizan or cavalry officer during the revolutionary war.

name: Havermeyer Wm. F.
biographical_note: Old German emigrant family.

name: Heard James
biographical_note: Of an ancient family of New Jersey are he and his brother Nicholas T. H.

name: Hedges Catharine
biographical_note: A tall, slender yankee, straight as a poplar tree, came hither to this Babel to seek his fortune. It was for a long time a desperate struggle between him and the grim-visaged fiend, poverty; nor did he better his condition by the active part he took in the political turmoils of the Bucktails and Clintonians, till meeting one day with a venerable maiden spinster, who was luxuriaving on the fat estates left her from her Rugers' relatives, he stepped into her good graces and fortune, and now dashes by "Crosby Castle" as proudly as any of the beneficiaries of that ancient property in the eastern suburbs of our city.

name: Hendricks Widow
biographical_note: Rich Jews.

name: Heyer Cornelius
biographical_note: Verv old New Yorkers.

name: Hicks Henry W.
biographical_note: Robert Hicks, Plymouth 1621, became a rich merchant. This was the progenitor of the family in Rhode Island, Long Island and New York. They have always "cottoned" to merchandise and been distinguished in the commercial annals of this country as shipping merchants at Plymouth, Newport and New York for 200 years past.

name: Hicks Silas
biographical_note: Rose to fortune with the late Mayor, C. W. Lawrence, in the auction business, and retired early to the precincts of Flushing, to enjoy his "otium cum dignitate." Of the family of Hicks, of Plymouth, (Mass.)

name: Hoffman L. M. (A German family)
biographical_note: His brother is As. V. Chancellor. The first Hoffman here was Anthony, a venerable and highly esteemed merchant, grandfather of L. M. H. Few families, for so few a number of persons as compose it have cut a 'larger swath' or "bigger figure" in the way of posts of preferment. Talent and also public services rendered, martial gallantry, poetry, judicial acumen, oratory, all have had their lustre mingled with this name. Beekman Verplanck Hoffman, a post captain; Judge Josiah O. Hoffman, and his sons Ogden and Charles, &c., all foremost men In our community.

name: Holbrook Ephraim
biographical_note: Connecticut, and rich by dry goods. &c.

name: Holmes Eldad
biographical_note: Old New Yorker, or very long here as a respectable merchant, of the old democratic school. Silas Holmes is a horse of a different color, and celebrated as the most unlucky dog in running vessels on reefs and rocks that ever commanded a ship.

name: Hone Philip
biographical_note: A worthy man, and with his brother John, famous auctioneers in their day and made great gains by it. They were sons of a German, a respectable baker by profession, whose hard earnings the boys turned to good account, so that Philip became for one year Mayor of the city, and a gay, jovial, capital Mayor he was, and a most enterprising, liberal citizen has he always been. Honesdale will tell a tale, to the future, how honorably his name associates with the Delaware & Hudson Canal. [See Anthon.]

name: Hosack (Estate of David)
biographical_note: Bút few names will shine long in the annals of medical history brighter than this. Whatever may be said of his foibles as a man, his superior practical talent, sagacity, boldness and decision as a bedside practitioner, and the invaluable lessons in medicine he has left will live forever while truth and common sense prevail. He was a man reckless and extravagant in money matters, and as he often said, his embarrassments were perpetually bringing him to the verge of bankruptcy. In latter years, happily or unhappily for him, a sudden tide of wealth poured profusely into his lap by his marriage with the rich widow of Henry A. Coster, (see Coster,) by which he realized the immediate possession of over half a million of personal property alone, that nearly turned and intoxicated his excitable brain and impetuous temperament. Converting that and portions of the large real estate income of Mrs. Coster into new purchases in his own name, he hoped to gvie a solid and permanent basis to the opulence with which he naturally and modestly designed to enrich his own chi'dren by a former wife. At Hyde Park he laid out his lawns and grounds with a luxuriousness that surpassed the English gentry, and spared no expense, transplanting often full grown trees from the sides of mountains to form readymade forest scenery upon his own plantation; together with this property be made large investments in insurance stocks, but in his avidity to become

Cræsus it seemed as if an evil star would indignantly rob him of all. The dreadful conflagration that laid 600 houses in ashes in one night, swallowed up nearly every insurance company in the city. The shock caused an apoplexy, and he perished; and since his death the terri- ble depression in real estate has in fine left his own chil- dren with but a few thousands-barely enough to support them, and even much of that has been melted away. So much for a self made man, whose father was a humble redemptioner that worked his apprenticeship out in the Roosevelt family, and became rich, and richly endowed his favorite son David, and then set him up in practice by purchasing out Dr. Samuel Bard. But for David's mar- riage with a widow of extreme wealth, he would have been now and for years hence at the head of his profes- sion, and of eminent usefulness-all of which and his life were suddenly arrested by the potent influence of gold.

name: Howell Mrs. Wm.
biographical_note: Was a daughter of the rich old Blackwell, an old New York family, and owner of Blackwell's Island at Hell Gate. Her husband a Howell or Hoel, old Long Island Suffolk name, and his niece and heir is Julia Dekay, of a very ancient New York family, and wife of Major Jack Downing No. 2, i. e. C. A. Davis, vide.

name: Howland G. G.
biographical_note: One of the special partners to the tune of $150,000 in the House of Howland & Aspinwall. He and his brother Sam obtained some notoriety by building vessels for the Greeks in their struggle for independence.

name: Hoyt Gould
biographical_note: Of a respectable Connecticut family, and long known as one of our oldest merchants, under the distinguished firm of Hoyt & Tom. A son of this retired "Rentier" married a daughter of President Duer, of Columbia Col- lege.

name: Hubbard Ν. Τ.
biographical_note: Ancient and honored name of New England.

name: Hudson Joseph
biographical_note: Respectable English importer for some years here, and has doubled his fortune by marrying a daughter of the rich Henry Laverty. He has no reason to regret having adopted for his home the capital which graces the noble river that bears his name in honor of its discoverer, and peradventure his ancestor, "Hendrick Hudson."

name: Hunt Jonathan
biographical_note: Reputed a millionaire and a protege of our parvenu cliques, who are by no means reluctant to give him the hand of fellowship, because Jonathan, like them, came from nothing. But it is a mystery how a person yet un- der forty, and who a very few years since was in the line of small groceries at Troy became suddenly at Mobile, as if by the magic of open sessame! into such surprising wealth as is imputed to him. The good old dames who hunt up fortunes for matches to their scarecrow daugh- ters, are as mad after our worthy apple merchant since he became reputed so rich as they have been for some time back to get hold of Chinese Whetmore. (See Whet- more.)

name: Hyslop Robert
biographical_note: An old New York family.

name: Irving Mrs. Jno. T. (husband's estate,)
biographical_note: Judge John T., deceased, and Washington Irving, so renowned, and William, Ebenezer, Peter, &c., are the sons of a respectable shoemaker (deceased) in William street, in this bleesed city of New York.

name: Jackson Hamilton
biographical_note: Inherits the large estate of Jno. Jackson, one of two brothers (John and Samuel) who early located at Brook- lyn, and became rich by the rise of real property. These two brothers Jackson were lineal descendants of Colonel Jno. Jackson, High Sheriff of Queen's County, Judge, &c. eldest son and heir of Robert and Agnes Jackson, among the first English settlers of Hempstead, L. I.-about 1654.

name: James Henry
biographical_note: Son of the rich Wm. James, of Albany, (deceased) and a gentleman celebrated, we believe, for his extensive scholarship and literary attainments.

name: Janeway (Estate)
biographical_note: The Janeway property comes chiefly by intermarriage through that most ancient and wealthy and respectable Dutch family Van Zandt, descendants maternally of Lord Peter Prauw.

name: Jay Wm.
biographical_note: Son of Gov. John Jay. See Jay.

name: Jay Peter A.
biographical_note: The first Jay on the Records appears to have been John Jay (probably a Hougenot), a quaker in the suite of Geo. Fox, in his journey through America 1671-2, and who meeting with a dislocation of his neck, was marvellously cured by the aforesaid George, somewhere in New Jer- sey, and thus by this miracle lived with his head on to be- come the head of an illustrious house.

name: Jennings Chester
biographical_note: Came a poor boy, a stage driver, from New England, and entering the door of the City Hotel with whip in hand, asked for work, was hired as a waiter, and by good conduct rose successively to the rank of head waiter, and afterwards, with his equally enterprising and famous fel- low-waiter, Willard, to copartner in that ancient establish- ment, where his fortune was thus honestly and honorably acquired-but badly singed by dipping in Biddle banking.

name: Johnson William Samuel
biographical_note: A highly respectable lawyer, prominent whig politi- cian, and late Alderman, and grand son of the former President Johnson, of Columbia College, a Connecticut family, and Wm. S. gets the mass of his fortune by mar riage with the daughter "Cardinal Woolsey," as this eminent merchant used facetiously to be called " 011 change." The "Cardinal" was an extensive operator in Connecticut banks, and became very rich.

name: Jones Edwd. R.
biographical_note: His father was Joshua, an eminent cooper, and thus ac- quired a large fortune, in connexion with Jas. Lennox (brother of Robert,) in the same business, immediately a- fter the revolution.

name: Judd Samuel
biographical_note: From "down east," and educated in the oil line, pro- bably among Cape Codders and Nantucketers, and thus having become familiar with blubber, sat up here a very small shop in the upper part of the city, and in which,

though not big enough to swing a cat in, he sold lamp wicks till he could build his own palaces and ships, and drive his own splendid carriage. The dazzling light of the purest sperm that makes a fairy land of his saloons and greenhouses on some gala right, must shine beautifully and soothingly on the vision of one who can truly say he is indebted to none but his own genius and hard toil for the gold which whale oil has brought to his house.

name: Kane Oliver
biographical_note: For many years a distinguished family in New York that has seen both much prosperity as well as the dark side of life's picture. One of the dashing young men of this family a few years since speculated largely in stocks and blew out his brains. But few retain the wealth they once had; most, however, are well intermarried with respectable families, being themselves a race decidedly of strong and prominent traits of intellect.

name: Keese John D.
biographical_note: Of the old established firm of Lawrence & Keese, who have during the last 50 yeaas sold drugs enough to supply half the human race. Mr. K. is a son of Major Keese, (deceased) of the continental line of the revolution, afterwards a distinguished lawyer in this city.

name: Kearney J. D. Jr (Estate of J. Watts)
biographical_note: Young grandson of Jno. Watts, deceased, and which is the source of his wealth. The Watts family are cotemporary with the most distinguished names of our early English gentry, John Watts being grandson of Robert and Mary Watts-and this Mary the daughter of the Speaker of the Provincial Assembly, Wm. Nicoll, the patentee of the Islip Manor, L. I. Jno. Watts' sister was mother of the present Mr. Kennedy, Earl of Cassilis.

name: Kennedy David
biographical_note: A highly respectable Scotch merchant, who obtained some addition to his fortune through his wife, the daughter of Robert Lennox.

name: Kent James
biographical_note: One of the most marked mea of the times, the profound jurist, and long the celebrated Chancellor of this State, and whose opinions and commentaries, nay, mere words, on all legal matters are themselves like pure gold and law for all who wish to know what law is. What a gratifying picture of a well-spent life is that of this universally beloved man in a green old age, enjoying the respect of friends, the delights of domestic society, and all the glorious vigor and sparkling brightness of that manhood which once adorned and could still adorn if not purify that bench, which would more deplore his loss but for the elevation to a most important judicial station of that able son for whose shoulders it would seem the father designed his own spotless mantle.

name: Kermit Robert
biographical_note: Of a very old and most respectable New York family.

name: Kernochan Joseph
biographical_note: Of a poor Irish family, who were employed as colliers, teamsters, &c., at some of the large iron works in the Highland Mountains, on the west side of the Hudson. Joseph went to the West Indies, and came back rich.

name: Ketchum Morris
biographical_note: Brother, we believe, of Hiram the lawyer, a New Yorker.

name: Kettletas Eugene
biographical_note: Of a very ancient New York family. The present worthy buffo tragedian, James H., (now since the death of his cousin) Baron or Lord Hackett, of Hackettstown, is maternally of the Kettletas line.

name: King James G.
biographical_note: Son of Rufus K. Rufus K. by his powerful mind rose great eminence in the councils of the nation. His parts were of humble origin, but few fathers have left sons whose ambition and towering pride in their "cherished parent," have together conspired to give so fictitious and forced an elevation and consequence to a family as this one has for years enjoyed. Yet, after all, their importance resolves itself in the isolated fact of the fortunate position which their father occupied, and which was not so peculiarly distinguished as to merit the laudation and glorification which by industrious and indefatigable trumpeting have been lavished on this individual, otherwise extremely obnoxious to the population at large by his aristocratic pretensions.

name: Kingsland R.
biographical_note: One of the oldest and richest firms in the hardware line, and a man of great respectability and high standing in this community, a modest gentleman that has calmly pursned "the even tenor of his way" without show or parade, and thus silently passed down the stream of time, every where beloved and honored. Such families, how unlike are they to the fluttering, buzzing things of fashion, whose gilded wings collapse with the first shower that dims their sunshine, and "then are heard no more."

name: Kissam Richard (Estate of)
biographical_note: An estate honestly, nobly acquired in the surgical profession of which he stood undoubtedly at the head in his day. But few keener men in wit or with the knife could be found any where. His forte was lithotomy, in which his cures were truly wonderful. In after life he married and soon after died, leaving a large family of small children, to whom we believe his property was by will bequeathed.

name: Knapp Shepard
biographical_note: From down East, and self made.

name: Kneeland Charles
biographical_note: From down East, and self made.

name: Laurie George
biographical_note: George and John L. for 30 years merchants, and bachelors, (originally from Scotland) and of the very first character and prime brand as to respectability. The universal consideration they enjoy in this community must be a comfort to them when they look back upon the bright and honorable career they have passed through, though neither, we hope, is yet too advanced into the yellow leaf to forswear and become a Benedict, which they both know they could easily do, and have only to say the word in these hard times, and Hymen stands ready to light his altar torch, and cupid to let fly from his quiver one of his most barbed arrows, that not even the tough texture of a bachelor's heart could resist.

name: Laverty Henry
biographical_note: The keenest, smoothest chap for small change in the way of talk and knowing every body, and every thing that can be found for the last 40 years. He holds on well and is as young and gay as when a boy he dealt out tape and bobbinet behind the counter, little dreaming then he should one day figure so large in Broadway, or that his saloons would be hung round with the productions of his own accomplished daughter's pencil. Some have thought her the heroine of Halleck's beautiful poem of "Fanny."

name: Lawrence John B.
biographical_note: A model-man of the old school gentlemen merchants is he. Of a very ancient house, of an old New York family, and though inheriting a large estate from his father, he pursued business with the ardor of youth, and doubled and trebled his property in the drug line, as the head of that celebrated firm, Lawrence, Keese & Co. But few such as he and the late Gen. Matthew Glarkson,

name: Lawrence C. W.
biographical_note: Of highly respectable quaker descent on all sides-rose to fortune in the Auctioneer house of Hicks, Lawrence & Co. Cornelius has been Mayor of the city.

name: Lawrence Isaac (Estate of)
biographical_note: The millionaire once so esteemed, has left, it is said, not a sous! How much has been eaten up in bad lot speculations?

name: Lawrence Joseph
biographical_note: Brother of Cornelius, and married a rich heiress, daughter of Ald. Thos. S. Townsend, of the Long Is- I and Townsends.

name: Lawrance Abrabam
biographical_note: Descended, with R. M. L. and most of the name in this city, including Cornelius, from three brothers from England, John, William and Thomas.

name: Leavitt David
biographical_note: From down East, and a merchant-an ultra tea total- ler, in which his zeal has made him conspicuous, though it has not been turned, perhaps, to a mercenary account as ingeniousiy as Arthur Tappan has trafficked with his incendiary abolition plots.

name: Leggett Thomas
biographical_note: The Leggetts are an old New York family, but chief- ly known as a very money-getting race. The one who was President of a certain rotten banking institution stands as to reputation, in a very equivocal light before the community.

name: Lenox James
biographical_note: Son of Robert, deceased, who was the British commis- sary to that charnel house to Americans, the Jersey pri- son ship, and thus ingloriously amassed a portion, at least, of his wealth. Robert was brother of James. (See Jones above.

name: LeRoy Jacob R.
biographical_note: Of an ancient and highly distinguished Hougenot fami- ly, we believe. Daniel Webster, the Sec. of State, mar- ried for his last and present wife a Miss Le Roy. Jacob Le Roy inherits a large estate from his father-in-law, Thos. Otis, who as Otis & Swan, in the war made a great deal of money.

name: Lewis Morgan
biographical_note: Formerly Gov. of the State, Major General of the Ar- my, &c. Acquired his estate by marrying a Livingston of wealth. Gen. Lewis is of an ancient Welsh family.

name: Little Jacob
biographical_note: An old and worthy citizen, and one the shrewdest brokers in Wall st.

name: Livingston Maturine
biographical_note: Though of the family of Livingston, which for a family so prolific and numerous as theirs is has been one of the most wealthy in the State, but little in the subdivision of multiplying generations fell to the share of Maturine, who marrying his cousin, daughter of Gen. Morgan Lewis, above, has however acquired large opulence in perspec- tive. The Livingstons began in this state about 150 years ince in the person of a Scotch clergyman, who on a fa- mous old white horse made his itinerations through the valley of the Mohawk tell with effect. From him sprang an intelligent, enterprising race, who in the next genera tion secured large landed estates, since manorial by their extent on both sides the Hudson River. Martin Van Bu- ren commenced his career as a village lawyer at Kinder- hook by undertaking to invalidate the Livingston titles, but their numbers and wealth were too much for him. Their names loom largely on our records; and after the Rensselaers and the Dutch and English noblesse, they rank among the most distinguished families in the State.

name: Lord Daniel
biographical_note: A most profound lawyer, it is said, and none the worse for his filial attention to and affection for, his venerable parent, "Doctor Lord." If one may rise to a practice of $20.000 per annum on the quirks and quibbles of legal technicalities, why may not another upon the mummery and mystifications of patent prescriptions in the healing art.

name: Lord Rufus L.
biographical_note: A demi millionaire and a bachelor. His large property is composed of a number of valuable stores in the burnt district.

name: Lorrillard Peter
biographical_note: The Lorillards are a Hougenot family, we believe, and of course of respectable origin, as all who fled hither for the glorious object of religious liberty, undoubtedly were. The brothers Jacob, Peter and George, the first as a lea- ther merchant, the other two as tobacconists, are famous in our city, especially the two last, for the prodigious wealth they have acquired by those callings. They en- tered early into large purchases of real estate; in the city, when there was no such thing as moonshine lots and Munchausen speculators.

name: Lovett James
biographical_note: A retired sea captain and afterwards a shipping mer- chant, originally from Rhode Island, and of the real grit that comes of late as of yore, from that little chivalrous spot that gave birth to a Perry and a Greene.

name: Low (Estate of) Nicholas
biographical_note: Of old respectability. and of the highest standing as a merchant, a halfa century gone, and as the proprietor of Sans Souci hotel at Balston Spa, in her palmy days, madę a princely fortune, which, but for the "moving of the waters" to Saratoga would have been doubled. The great American pedestrian, Colonel Nicholas, L., who won the recent wonderful wager, we believe, of 1000 miles in 1000 hours, spite of the sneers of his pretended backers, at the Union Club, is a worthy son of Nicholas Low. Mr. Chas. A. King married a daughter, and thereby inherited a large fortune.

name: Low Daniel
biographical_note: Another enterprising, driving, smooth-faced, pleasant son of New England, who resided a long time, as a mer- chant, in Paris, and surmounting every blasting sirocco in trade, came out rich, and spread largely into real estate.

name: Ludlow (Estate)
biographical_note: For a century and more one of the most distinguished and wealthy of New York families-now dwindled in numbers and fortune, but by marriage engrafted on va- rious other excellent families.

name: Luff (Estate of) John
biographical_note: A most capital man was this respectable German, and in the excellent quality of his bread and muffin.
