Jonas Humbert himself, aided by his electrical machine, was no circumstance to him. Old Mr. Luff had a pleasant word for every one as he rattled around from door to door in The and men of that high stamp and tone are now left. halls of our public charities tell of their benevolent deeds and the hours they have stolen from busy life to de- vote to their duties to the poor and suffering, to relieve the widow and the orphan and to wipe the tear of sorrow from misery's pallid cheek.

his long light baker's cart, which it seems to us is now become of the things that only once were, and are found no more among our modern landaus and Berlins. With a becoming pride his children revert back with pleasure to the honest means by which their revered parent made, by his own hands, so large an estate.

name: McBride James
biographical_note: An Irish gentleman, who by a steady, upright, straightforward course of trade in the dry goods line, consolidating by his unblemished and pure life troops of fast friends around him, has amassed a vast property.

name: McFarlane (Estate of) Henry
biographical_note: A poor boy, who rose first to a clerk, then to be partner in the old house of Blackwell, iron merchants.

name: McLean Hugh, M.D.
biographical_note: A worthy "medico," and now one of the fathers of the profession; a gay Lothario of an old bachelor is he, and a "virtuoso" in music and the fine arts; yet strange to say has not, as many have, thereby lost caste among the faculty, but always has had a great run of practice. Much of this good luck is traceable to the fact that his worthy mother, Mrs. Glass, was the most popular midwife among all the gentry of New York in revolutionary days, as her advertisement in Rivington's old Tory Gazette, while the "Britishers" were here, clearly shows.- She could do much among the petticoats to forward her son's prospects.

name: Macoun Wm. T.
biographical_note: Vice Chancellor, and from the rank of a young attorney who came from the country to practice law, and try his fortune here, has risen, step by step, by his own merits, into the good opinion of the profession and community till honored with one of the highest dignities of the State.

name: Macy Josiah
biographical_note: From the Cape Cod region.

name: Maitland R.
biographical_note: Scotch, and some of his wealth comes through his wife, daughter of Robt. Lennox. See Lennox above.

name: Marks Mrs. (?)
biographical_note: The husband of Mrs. Marks was a rich Jew from the South. She is we believe of the ancient Dutch family of Beekmam (vide) of this city. Her son is a gay youth and drives the neatest turn-outs of any of our beau monde.

name: Marshall Benjamin
biographical_note: One of the earliest who boldly enterprised American manufacturing establishments, and by them has managed, strange to say, to obtain large profits and wealth.

name: Mason (Estate of) John
biographical_note: Another practical exemplification of the rich rewards of mechanic industry. John was a first rate tailor in his day, and from cutting cloth proceeded to the rank of "Merchant Tailor and Draper," and by vending piece goods, and turning over and over again his profits, and vesting in real estate when cheap, left near a million- and to his credit gave his family an excellent education; one daughter marrying a Jones, son of a cooper by trade, (brother of Edward R. J. above), and therefore keeping properly to her own grade, and now at the very tip top pinnacle of that peculiar class of dashing, fashionable people. A son of Jno. Mason married the charming young actress, Miss Wheatley, of the Park Theatre, talented daughter of her famous, talented mother, Mrs. W., who is still the "Mademoiselle Mars" of that old establishment. The Mason family are one of those exсерtions to the common rule of parvenues in our city, that they have the good sense never to decry mechanics and tradespeople from whom they sprang, but rather court them, and give them the entree to their brilliant soirees, where the properties of steam engines, the virtues of hickory hoop-poles, and good oak staves, and the last cuts and patterns of the costumes of Bond street and the Boulevards are discussed with like philosophical acuinen and minuteness.

name: Mauran Oroondates
biographical_note: Formerly engaged in the - - - - trade, and made money also by the steam ferry between Havana and - - - - It is supposed that he has lost largely by the failure of the extensive house of Geo. Knight & Co., of Havana.

name: Maxwell Hugh
biographical_note: One of the ablest lawyers and first of orators at the bar: his father was a respectable Scotchman, and a brewer at Baltimore, and Hugh married the beautiful daughter of an eminent blacksmith of this city. Now their son is Secretary of Legation at Petersburg! Such is the reward of merit.

name: Mesier P. A. Jr.
biographical_note: Of the old Knickerbocker stamp, and a worthy flour merchant or bookseller his father was, we forget which.

name: Milderberger Christian
biographical_note: Of the German emigrant colonization that got a good foothold here about a century since, when property could be bought for a mere song.

name: Miller John G.
biographical_note: Rich and rides iu his equipage, but, it is said, sprang from a very obscure origin, we believe that of a "candle-snuffer" in the Park Theatre. So much the more honor to him, to have made such a leap as he has. He need not shun investigation into his pedigree, nor cry with Othello, "Put out the light!" for it was truly the light and life of his vocation, and the source of his fortune.

name: Minturn R. B.
biographical_note: There are those who have seen pass before them in the panorama of the last half century, a succession of reigning houses of wide spread fame as India merchants, all now toppled down one after the other, and gone to perdition! This Minturn is one of the sons of that Minturn who, with the Franklins and Robinsons, were among the very earliest to launch out their 700 ton ships for the China seas, these ships then being deemed Mammoths in naval architecture. How have things changed, when 1000 tons is scarcely looked at for an ordinary packet!

name: Monroe Mrs. James (Douglass Estate)
biographical_note: Wife of Capt. or Col Jas. Monroe, formerly of the army, and nephew of the late James Monroe, President of the United States, whose ancestor, he boasted in telling, was a tanner.

name: Moore Clement C.
biographical_note: Of the highly respectable family of the late Bishop. Moore, whose ancestors located first at Newtown, L. I.,as plain farmers or mechanics, as most of the first colonists from England were. Clement is son of the venerable and revered Bishop Moore, deceased, of the Episcopal Couch and nephew of the much beloved and distinguished physician, Dr. Wm. Moore, deceased.

name: Morgan John I.
biographical_note: Rich and of no calling, as we know, but has been a po- litical man, and in high trusts, and in the midst of party strife-always courteous and amiable. A worthy man.

name: Morris Gouverneur
biographical_note: His father, the venerable and famous Gouverneur M., late in life married a Randolph, of Virginia, and left this the only inheritor; richly, as Martin Wilkins and other expectants said, meriting the name of Kut.us off at that zime a great Russian warrior. The Morris family of New York and New Jersey began on a large figure, and have so continued to prosper for 160 years. Col. Lewis Mor- ris, a celebrated English quaker merchant of Barbadoes, and friend of Wm. Penn, coming here to New York with his own ships and goods, and with his brother, Captain Richard M., making immediately purchases of large tracts on Long Island, at Harlem river, at Shrewsbury, &c., N. J. (hence Morristown and Morrisana estate, the last the es- tate of the youngster above,) &c. And from this truly illustrious stock came all the Morrises hereabout and in New Jersey; and in their hands the patrimonial estates still rest, together with the household jewelry and plate for many generations back, which few families can say.

name: Morse Sidney E.
biographical_note: An editor, and son we believe, of the famous geogra- pher, the Rev. Jedediam Morse.

name: Mortimer Richard
biographical_note: An honest upright tailor, now retired on a large estate. His lovely daughter was deemed, beyond all question, the reigning belle at Saratoga-not even eclipsed by Miss Jones; whose grandpa was alike a self made man from the shopboard. [Vide Mason.]

name: Mott Valentine Dr.
biographical_note: A famous surgeon-of an old respectable quaker fami- v of English descent-the progenitor locating about two centuries since at Mespath Kills, Newtown, afterwards at Hempstead. Valentine is the architect of his own for- tunes, and has literally cut his way up to a very hand- some estate. Viva la scalpel! The other Motts here are of the same stem.

name: Moulton Charles
biographical_note: Charles was an active shrewd little broker some 15 years since in Wall street, and by some fortunate pur- chase of real estate became wealthy. He married for love a pretty little poor girl, the much accomplished only daughter of a respectable German piano teacher by a French wife. Mademoiselle Metz-now Mad. Moulton -was in early life deemed almost a musical prodigy by her voice and performances on the piano, and brought money to her parents by singing at public concerts. Moulton is of a high New England family, and has resi- ded some years since in Paris, where Madame Moulton has made great exertions to be received into "haute so- ciete," and finally succeeded in being permitted to play the part of a "Pompadour Sheperdess" in one of the tableaux recently given at a bail of the Duke of Orleans. We do not think it possible that any American lady with American blood in her veins, would ever demean herself and expose herself to the sneers and sarcasms of the "Fauxbourg St. Germaine," for the sake of being occa- sionally favored with a haughty nod of recognition from any of the ancient noblesse or new made peers of the French kingdom. Nous verrons.

name: Munn Stephen B.
biographical_note: One of the old-st dry goods merchants of New York- long a famous house in Pearl street. We believe him a worthy man, but close-fisted and grasping.

name: Murray John R.
biographical_note: The Murrays were about half a century since among the most wealthy and influential, and hail from noble Scotch extraction, though the most eminent here have been of the Society of Friends. One of these latter, on the high seat, set up his carriage, which, being deemed a little too luxurious, he palliated the censure by calling it a leathern vehicle" for convenience! Col. Jas. P. M. in later times, was a conspicuous merchant, and married a daughter of Dr. Bronson. [VideBronson.]

name: Neilson (Estate of) Wm.
biographical_note: An ancient merchant of very great repute and wealth, and long deceased, married "Lady Kitty Duer," widow of the former John Duer, and daughter of Lord Sterling, of the Continental army. By "Lady Kitty" or Catharine, old Mr. Neilson left a numerous family, one of whom, William, a respectable merchant and much esteemed gentleman, married a daughter of John B. Coles, de- ceased, and thus added to his fortune.

name: Nevius P. I.
biographical_note: Of an ancient and highly respectable Dutch family of our olden time, and acquired his fortune in the flour line.

name: Newbold George
biographical_note: A merchant. The Newbolds are of a very ancient and highly distinguished family of New Jersey.

name: Niblo Wm.
biographical_note: The matchless and incomparable Niblo, proprietor of of the Niblo Gardens, director of operas, vaudevilles, &c. &c. ad infinitum. An English boy, and began friendless, as a waiter, then became lord and master of a famous game hotel corner of Cedar street-finally expanded his wings to a higher flight, and branching into every species of elegant refinement that could pamper the public taste and palate to boot, bas become decidedly the most promi- nent man that ever flourished in this good city, in the way of getting up agreeable and entertaining amusements of every variety, and sp'endid festivals, banquetings, &c. &c. In his line he is decidedly one of the "Heads of the People."

name: Norris Thomas P.
biographical_note: Ofa very old and respectable Dutch family.

name: Ogden J. D.
biographical_note: Jno. Ogden. farmer, 1644, appears as one of the four patentees of Hempstead, L. I., and came from England. This appears to be the root of the highly respectable Og- dens of New York and New Jersey.

name: Olmstead Francis
biographical_note: A worthy fellow and one of the few instances of a prosperous merchant retiring at the right time. He is of the land of "steady habirs" and cousin of the very dis- tinguished Prof. O., of Yale.

name: Packard Isaac
biographical_note: Sundry "haciendas" and negro plantations in Cuba point darkly to the rather dubious track in which this adventurous New Englander to the tropics soon became by the characteristic cupidity of his countrymen, warmed into a West Indian temperament and a ready prosolyte to the ways of getting money in the Spanish colonies.

name: Paine John
biographical_note: A youth well esteemed, and only child of a rich father
who got his money by hard knocks. But no family of Vermont nobility can hold up their heads higher than this.

name: Parmelee Dr.
biographical_note: The accomplished dentist and a demi-millionaire by his success in this art backed by a keen relish for, and "cute" Yankee tact in turning a penny. He is the richest of all this numerous profession and lives in princely style. The best patrons of dentists in this country are our mercurial teeth-destroying doctors.

name: Payne Thatcher T.
biographical_note: Served a severe apprenticeship to struggling up-hill labor, as a school teacher, and became of great eminence as a linguist and correct scholar-being of a family part Jew, from the east end of Long Island, not far from Montauk. Is brother of the justly celebrated Jno. Howard Payne. Thatcher, however, in fortune has taken the wind out of the sails of the wandering poet, who has as much to do as ever to get his crust for Thatcher now has his liveries and his valets, and drives his carriage, and lives in snuff "per force" of a most capital speculation he made in marrying the rich young blooming widow of Mr Baily, a rich merchant, dec'd, that left a plumb nearly to her, and we hope Thatcher, who is a lucky rogue, don't forget his poor relations, if he has any

name: Pearsall Thomas
biographical_note: Merchant, distant relative of Thomas, deceased.

name: Post Allison
biographical_note: The progenitor of the Posts was an humble mechanic, among the early English settlers of Suffolk co., L 1, and thence the family soon after located at Hempstead, Queens co. Joel and Jotham Post (both deceased), brothers of Allison, were, together with the late distinguished Dr. Wright Post, (another brother) sons of a highly respectable butcher. Wright's early lessons in the shambles gave him, probably, his strong taste for and afterwards eminence in, anatomy. Joel and Jotham, about 30 years since, carried on a great stroke in the drng line; then smashed; but a few years after built a magnificent store and warehouse, &c; launched larger than ever into the vending of apothecary stuffs, and, together with Waldron B., (son of one of the parties) accumulated a very large estate, on which their families are now luxuriating in the fauxbourgs of our new made quality in the vicinity of Upper Broadway.

name: Post Waldron B.
biographical_note: To his fortune as above acquired Waldron added a considerable amount by marriage with a Miss De Wolfe, of Rhode Island. The De Wolfs are several of them Cuba planters, and one made a vast estate by trafficking in the slave trade.

name: Prall David M.
biographical_note: An ancient and respectable New York family in the the mercantile line.

name: Prime Edward
biographical_note: Son of the "doctor," as his rich deceased father, Nathaniel, the Wall st. broker so famous, was called. What the soubriquet of doctor had reference to, except to certain veterinary reminiscences connected with Nathaniel's early reputed occupations, we know not. However, he seems to have become an adept in something more than horse-flesh, and learned how to crack a good bargain in the way of money changes and brokerages, in which line, at New York, he rose by persevering industry to be the head and founder of the celebrated banking house of Prime, Ward & King, &c.

name: Palmer John 1.
biographical_note: One of New England's numerous enterprising sons who have found New York the most successful field for their monetary operations.

name: Parish Henry (His brother)
biographical_note: This family sprang from ar honorable root, a surgeon of the Britisn Navy, who about two centuries ago located in this province. A romantic incident connects with Dr. Parish:-In one of the earliest commercial adventures from a neighboring village to the south, and in which a venerable lady, the proprietor of the vessel and her cargo of cider and apples, went passenger, accompanied by a beautiful daughter, Dr. Parish also was invited to act as navigator. At Ocracoke Inlet they saw the head of a celebrated pirate nailed to the bowsprit of a vessel of war; and, on their return, were overtaken by a storm which, but for the doctor's seamanship, would have consigned them all to the deep. For this he received the hand of the fair young damsel on board, and thus became a denizen of this province But from that day to this, the generations have never been blessed with worldly prosperity until in the persons of Henry and his brothers.

name: Paulding William
biographical_note: Former mayor, &c., of the democratic school, and alleged descendant of Paulding, one of the captors of the British spy Major Andre. That sterling continental soldier little imagined that one of his descendants would become enriched by intermarriage with one of the rankest tory families of the revolution-to wit, a Rhinelander.- [See below.]

name: Pearsall Mrs. Thos.
biographical_note: Her husband, of an old Long Island family of Hempstead, inherited a large estate from his father, a quaker merchant, and this was doubled by his alliance with this la ty, daughter of the rich Scoth merchant, Thomas Buchanan.

name: Peck Elisha
biographical_note: The Pecks are from a highly respectable and very ancient English family, who first came to Boston, then located at Saybrook, Conn.

name: Peters John R.
biographical_note: Of an ancient and respectable N. Y. family. Alderman Jno. R., deemed one of the "cutest" politicians of his time in the democratic ranks.

name: Phelps Thaddeus
biographical_note: The Phelpses come from Connecticut and are highty respectable.

name: Phoenix J. P.
biographical_note: Formerly a grocer, the standing whig candidate for Mayor; he is a son in-law of Stephen Whitney.

name: Pierson Henry L.
biographical_note: Iron merchant, and son of Jeremiah, who with Isaac P, established in the very infancy of our manufactures, a nail, and afterwards in adddition a cotton factory, on the Ramapo river, and there acquired great wealth. Isaac, until of late years, resided in the city, and held places of public trust, which he filled with great credit as a prominent leader in the "old guard" of the democracy of the Jefferson school. The progenitor of the Piersons was a clergyman and pastor of the English Colony that founded Southampton, Suffolk Co. two centuries ago.

name: Rankin (estate of Henry)
biographical_note: The Rankins are among the old Knickerbockers.

name: Rathbone John Jr.
biographical_note: The Rathbones are Yankees from Conrcticut, we believe.

name: Ray Robert
biographical_note: Son of Cornelius R., an ancient merchant and old

Dutch New York family. Robert added some to his wealth by marring a daughter of N. Prime, the broker, Jno. A. King per contra, got a very large slice of the Ray property by marrying a daughter of Cors. Ray.

name: Reed Luman
biographical_note: Began as a modest grocer and accumulated great wealth. Before his decease was one of the most liberal patrons of our native artists, (Mount and others) and established a superb picture gallery, &c., that might compare with that of an English nobleman's.

name: Remsen Henry
biographical_note: The Remsens are one of our very oldest Dutch Knickerbocker famalies.

name: Rapilye G. (Estate of)
biographical_note: The first born Dutch child on Long Island, over two hundred years ago, was a Rapilye, at He ancient patrimonial estate somewhere near the Wallabout, (now the United States Navy Yard.) See Chabert.

name: Rhinelander Bernard
biographical_note: The Rainalders, for fifty years pas', among the richest of the rich in this city, were but humble tai ors and shoemakers in the revolution. The tories who staid in th city feathered their nests under the protection of the British f[?]g. Many of them ergaged in taking shares in privateering on the rebet merchant ships-and thus were huge fortunes made by the native born enemies of our independence out of the hard earnings of suffering families of patriotic whigs thus reduced to ruin.

name: Riker Richard
biographical_note: This celebrated criminal Judge, and so long Recorder, is from one of our most ancient Dutch families-the progenitor locating himself near Hell Gate on Riker's Island, so well known as the stopping place to small craft before they enter this modern Styx at unfavorable tides. If the ancestor fixed himself at the entrance of the Infernal Regions, it may be said of the offspring above, that he was legitimately the three-headed Cerberus who has guarded the gates and keys to those dungeons and penitentiaries which open to criminals on earth a temporary purgatory. Richard is a veritable knight of the old school; a preux chevalier-has shot his man in duello in the heats growing out of the great democratic Jefferson triumph of 1800, and in recherche politesse and refined courtesy of manner he is decidedly the most fascinating man that ever figured so high and so long in all the bitterest broils of party excitement for the last 40 years. He possesses that true art of the gentleman which in truth is nature, the "ars celare artem," and in whom the perpetual smile of serenity, and gentle suavity of manner never appear assumed or designed, but sincere and heartfelt. This, with a brilliant, strong intellect, has given him his peculiar power and ascendancy as the first political leader of his day.- Though subtle in diplomacy, no false pity or disparaging sinister thought could ever dilute or reverse his irrevocable and luminous decisions on the bench.

name: Roberts Daniel
biographical_note: His father a physician who acquired a fortune in the West Indies. Daniel is a lawyer.

name: Romaine Samuel B.
biographical_note: His father, Benjamin, acquired his fortune by speculation in water-lots, then the old "Collect," in Centre and Canal streets. He was true blue Tammany bucktail, and the man who got up the great funeral to the charnel-house at the Wallabout, of the 11,500 dead of the Jersey prison-ship.

name: Rodgers John R. B. (Estate of)
biographical_note: A respectable physician deceased, whose fortune in life was aided by a wealthy father, long a respectable Presbyteri în clergyman. Dr. R. doubled his fortune by holding the Health Office when it yielded $25,000 a year, and again by marrying the rich widow Smith. So the Doctor's children, if they had not all his intellect, were born with the advantage of being the inheritors of his fortune, which does much for a man's reputation in this country

name: Rogers Dr. J. Smyth
biographical_note: The wealthiest and most "distinguee" of all the Rogers famiiy of this city, was Moses, a smart little tailor of Stamford, Conn., that itinerated all along the villages of the Sound during the revolution, and followed in the train of the Suttlers of the British troops wherever they were quartered. Moses, however, with his brothers Henry and Nehemiah, had, like their brother Snip of the Rhinelander family, too vaulting an ambition and too great a thirst for money in those adventurous and troublous times to be confined to the goose and thimble, and therefore did they embark their awl in the privateering business.- Thence Nehemiah's promotion to a red-coat in the British line, and hence the wealth of many tories is much of it legitimately belonging to ruined whig families. Yet how the tide of fortune capriciously sets-two of the granddaughters of Moses have successively married to Willim, the second son of the rich patroon of Albany, Stephen Van Rensellaer, deceased, and the inheritance of their children is a princely estate.

name: Rogers Mrs. John
biographical_note: Was a Smith, widow af a rich merchant she is now the widow of Dr. Rodgers, dec'd.

name: Roosevelt Jas. I.
biographical_note: No family shine more honorably in the ancient Durch annals of this province than the Roosevelts-the venerated Burgomasters of their day.

name: Raggles Sam'l B.
biographical_note: A lawyer, of a respectable family of this state Samuel has dipped largely in real estate speculations, to his great regret no doubt, and has figured as an eminent financier among whig politicians-making a very narrow escape from the fraternal embraces of the "pipe layers."

name: Russell Chas. H.
biographical_note: A dashing New England merchant, one of the "Haute classe" of the would be rulers of fashion and polite cırcles.

name: Saltus Francis
biographical_note: He and "Old Nick," or the General, as they call his brother, who has been a fixture at the City Hotel coteries of old bachelors for half a century gone, are sons of a respectable sea-captain, dec'd, of old Dutch Knickerbocker extraction. Nicholas and Franeis are iron merchants. "Gin'ral" Nicholas has survived several generations of the old boys of the olden time, and is still as bright as a morning lark, and a most inveterate and impassioned admirer of "vimmen and vine." Who has not heard him recount his exploits in Russia, his intimacy with the Emperor Nicholas, his namesake, and above all, listened to his enchanting warbling of "Sweet Lullaby!"

name: Salles (Estate of) L.
biographical_note: A native of France, and upright merchant, who by cautious loans during great pressures, amassed rapidly overgrown opulence, stinting himself in his perepatetic perigrinations on "change" to pockets of bread and cheese,

and hoarding the doubloons for those who, by intermarriage with his children, will soon find ways and means to scatter much of it into useful circulation. The orphan children of Mr. Vail, late American Consul at Nantes or L'Orient, were consigned by the late Secretary of the Treasury, Mr. Crawford, to the protection of Mr.Van Buren, and through the latter's very prodigal patronage, they were thrown into positions to command eligible intermarriages, and thus their fortunate connection with the family of Mr. Salles,

name: Saltus Nicholas
biographical_note: (Vide preceding)

name: Sampson Joseph
biographical_note: A young man and a widower; the purchaser of S. Ward's house for the sum of $60,000. Has made his money in the auction business.

name: Sands Joseph
biographical_note: Highly respectable proprietor, who early settled on Long Island, and hence Sands' Point on the entrance of the Sound.

name: Schiefflin H'y C.
biographical_note: There is but this one family of the name, being descended from a German officer of the British army in the revolution, who while quartered here became enamoured with and married to a rich and beautiful heiress of an American family.

name: Schermerhorn Peter
biographical_note: The Schermerhorns are Dutch or German, and have risen to note within about a century past, as mechanics, small tradesmen, merchants, &c., keeping aloof from all entanglements of party strife or otherwise, and closely husbanding the abundant fruits of their laborious toil, which they have doubled by frequent alliances with other opulent families, the Jones, &c., of their own grade.

name: Sharp Peter
biographical_note: Sharpe & Sutphen made their money honestly by vending whips and cowhides of every denomination, when horse and ox flesh were in greater repute than they have been since they have been driven off the course by the fire horses of steam. Peter Sharpe was once a great man in the old Democratic ranks, and became Speaker of the Assembly.

name: Simers William L.
biographical_note: About 30 years ago the most famous nostrum vender and quack advertiser that Cherry street could boast of. He was the "Salus populi" Dr. Horne & Evans of that day, as many a poor Jack Tar along the ship yards and quay could testify and say.

name: Smith Edmund (deceased)
biographical_note: An ancient merchant of the "Bull Smith" branch of the legions of Smith. Edmund's ancestor was Patentee of Smithtown, Suffolk county, and an illustrious name in our early annals.

name: Stagg John P.
biographical_note: Of an old Knickerbocker race.

name: Stephens Benjamin
biographical_note: A wealthy New York merchant in the grocer line, and father of the present renowned traveller, who was brought up in the midst of muscovados and molasses, but his roaming spirit could not long endure such a pr son, and Palestine and Palenque are carved on his shield.

name: Stevens Horatio G.
biographical_note: Brother of "Alderman Sam," William, John, &c.- Their father Maj. Gen. Ebenezer Stevens, was a meritorious and gallant officer of the old continental line of the army of the revolution, and as Major commanded the artillery with deadly effect in several bloody encounters.- How honorable to Major Stevens to rise to that point from out of the ranks where it is said he enlisted as a private soldier, leaving his tools as a journeyman carpenter to take up the sword and battle axe for liberty. His sons have many of them inherited much of his inborn energy and power of mind. The Stevenses so celebrated in engineering, and sons of Col. Stevens, of Hoboken, are a totally different family.

name: Stevens Alex. H.
biographical_note: A surgeon of some repute, and son of General Ebene zer S. The Doctor's three successive marriages to rich heiresses has, we imagine, put more money in his purse than amputating tumors or tying up arteries.

name: Stevens Jno. C.
biographical_note: Robert L. and John C. are sons of Col. Stevens, deceased, of Hoboken. The eminent ability of Robert, as one who alone has inherited the mantle of his friend Fulton, is too well known to need remark.

name: Steward John
biographical_note: But 30 years a resident here, and by the force of his own straight-forward, clear-headed sagacity in the dry goods line, &c., has acquired near half a million.

name: Stewart Robert
biographical_note: One of two Scotch brothers, who, by marriage inherit the great old Dutch estate of the Lispenards, near Canal street.

name: Stuart R. L.
biographical_note: Noble as the "royal house of Stuart," is none we believe but this sweet gentleman whose steam candy trade has blown him and his up into bloated affluence, adopts the true version-being a true born Scot that can now support his title.

name: Storm Garret
biographical_note: Of an old Knickerbocker race.

name: Strong George W.
biographical_note: Of a Long Island family, of great respectability. The brother Benjamin, a very demure and over pious man in religious professions, but a bitter old Federalist was he in the politics of the olden time.

name: Strong Mrs. James
biographical_note: She was a Remsen, we think, and from hence her great estate.

name: Stuyvesant Peter G.
biographical_note: His ancestor, Governor-General and Admiral Von Peter Stuyvesant, that redoubtable little fiery gentleman, whose portraiture is so graphically touched by Diedrich Knickerbocker, and who, as the last of the Dutch dynasty, went out uproariously, a true martinet, subjecting all his vassals to courts martial, military flogging, &c., if they but dared to look at his surliness, is so familiarly known

in the history of New York, that this line suffices. Gov. Nicoll, from England, who brought the little gentleman to his bearings, omitted one thing, viz: to make him disgorge some of his cruel exactions, but Peter took the oath to the Gorgon banner of St. George, and doffed the beaver-tail and windmill escutcheon of Dutch authority, and thus retaining his rich cabbage gardens at Corlær's Hook and Bowery, hid himself away as snug as a mouse in a cheese. The generations from him are all baptized in the lace shirt in which he was christened. They inherit and keep too, the SILVER Spoon.

name: Suffern Thos.
biographical_note: A respectable Irish gent-nephew of the venerable Judge S., of Rockland Co., N. Y., deceased. The present Judge Edward Suffern, 1st Judge of Rockland Co., is son of the deceased Judge. Thomas owes his gold to o-bacco-that precious weed.

name: Suydam Fer'd S.
biographical_note: A worthy and highly respectable old New York family, of Dutch extract, we believe, and have plodded along, minding their own business and heaping up wealth, and meddling with no one.

name: Swan Benj'n L.
biographical_note: The firm of Otis & Swan were peculiarly lucky in their commercial arrangements during the last war.

name: Swords James
biographical_note: Eminent booksellers many years past. The best literary speculation one of them made was his intermarriage with a Lorillard.

name: Talbot C. Ν.
biographical_note: The most distinguished Talbots were of the same family as Com. Talbot, an honored naval hero of our country.

name: Tallmadge James
biographical_note: The "General," and once Lieut. Gov., and eminent as a jurist, senator, patron of American industry, &c. &c. "Good wine needs no bush:

name: Targee John
biographical_note: A young French adventurer, silversmith by trade, emigrated, some fifty years ago, to this country, and by good conduct and industry, and great shrewdness as a polici-cian in the democratic ranks, to which he, as it turned out, wisely attached himself, rendered himself eminentuy conspicuous in that party, and for his untiring devotion to their interest, was ichty rewarded with sundry profitable posts of honor. So distinguished had he become from the Jeffersonian triumph of 1800 upward, that Vice President Tompkins made him his confidential friend, and he was every where looked upon for a time as the most influential leader, if not chier of the party in this quarter of the state. Hence during the struggles to supplant Clinton, the poet Croaker wrote thus- "I'm sick of General Jackson's toast, Canals are nought to me; Nordo I care who rules the roast, Clinton or John Targee."

name: Taylor Moses
biographical_note: A very worthy man and grocer. His connection in bu siness with the Astors has brought gold to his coffers.

name: Thompson Jonathan
biographical_note: An apostle of the "old guard" of democracy, and comes from that vigorous nursery of such material, "010

name: Thorne Herman
biographical_note: This is the "Colonel," the very pink and glass of fash-fon in the Parisian circles. His old quaker ancestors of Flushing and Cow Neck would open their eyes to enter his gorgeous private chapel at his imperial mansion in the French capital. What changes in the wheel of fortune, from an humble purser in the navy? But Herman can fall back to earliest English history for the high rank of his ancestry, whatever the world may think of his fashionable follies. He begins, we hear, to sicken of the heart lessness of the life he is leading.

name: Tillou F. R.
biographical_note: A self-made man in the law; his father being a long time in the humble capacity of one of the Mayor's police marshals. This son married a sister of that remarkable genius and first of American poets, Dr. Joseph Rodman Drake-" Croaker senior."

name: Titus Wm. M.
biographical_note: Of an old quaker family of Long Island.

name: Todd Wm. W.
biographical_note: Of a New York family we believe; long distinguished democratic merchants.

name: Tonnellee John
biographical_note: French-kept a large glove store in Pearl street.

name: Townsend Thomas J.
biographical_note: Of the firm of T. J. and E. Townsend, dry good mer-chants, and self-made men. They are from Queen's Co., L. I., originally.

name: Townsend John R.
biographical_note: A highly respected member of the bar, and only son of Ald. Thoms S. T., deceased,

name: Townsend Elihu
biographical_note: Broker; of a New Haven family. He and his brother-in-law, Nevins, have amassed a large property.

name: Treadwell Adam
biographical_note: The Treadwells are an English family of great respec-tability who settled first at Ipswich, Mass., about two centuries ago.

name: Tucker Gideon
biographical_note: Self-made man. A mechanic and architect.

name: Tucker Fanning C.
biographical_note: This is truly a "tall" good fellow in every sense, being near seven feet in his shoes, as is plain to all men's views, sings an admirable song, and patronizes music and the opera, drives a fine team, and in short, is a first rate gentleman, living as a gentleman should, and showing that one can be such without neglecting even the severer enjagements of business and the counting room. For, where is the better and richer merchant than he among the whole catalogue of shippers? Prot. John B. Beck married a daughter of Mr. Tucker.

name: Van Allen James I.
biographical_note: A shrewd old Knickerbocker, formerly from Kinder-hook; made his large property in the dry goods trade in times when great profits and small risks were the order of the day.

name: Van Arsdale Peter, Dr.
biographical_note: A highly respected physician, who has by dint of se-vere and continued hard labor in his profession, ac-quired, in the upper part of our city, where the pay is
small, but sure, a comfortable fortune. He is of the old Dutch families.

name: Van Ransellaer (Estate of) Stephen
biographical_note: The late Patroon Van Rensellaer, of Albany, the lord of the manor Rensellaerwyck, the most ancient and distinguished name of the old Dutch gentry who came hither shortly after the discovery of the Hudson river 1609. The first Patroon or Lord Van Ransellaer, owned near thirty miles square, both sides the river, at, and above, and below Albany; the estate then comprehending that city, then a fortress, now a free town and capital of the State while the manor and all its feudal privileges, and the Helderberg mountains and its hardy tenants still do homage to the "noble house of Van Ransellaer. No family in America has so long kept together an estate to be compared with this in value, extent and princely hereditaments. Next to John Jacob Astor's it is the wealthiest in the country. Besides the "lordship" the late Patroon owned hundreds of lots in New York city; among others the block where Niblo has his garden. This family, generation after generation, have almost always leaned to the cause of popular rights, and in the revolution staked all. Fortune has ever smiled on them, and they in turn fully content with their most abundant allotment in the prizes of this world, have had no grasping desires for power and office; and like the present young Patroon, "young Steve," as his tenantry call him, let the world wag on in its own way, so they are let to hold fast to their own, however much the horn-blowers on the Helderberg may beleaguer the young Patroon and begrudge him the ownership of these posssessions, and threaten him with a breakfast of cold slugs, if he insist on the rent-roll.

name: Vandervoort Peter
biographical_note: No more respected merchant or citizen of exemplary purity of life than this head of the ancient and respectable house of Vandervoort & Flandin, whose once gay, fancy store, on the corner of Trinity church yard, is now replaced by the not less fashionable Restaurant which rejoiceth in the not unappropriate name, (for such a locality) "The Kremlin." Mr. Vandervoort owns the Kremlin house, and the family are one of the most ancient of the Dutch of this city.

name: Vanderbilt Cornelius
biographical_note: Of an old Dutch root. Cornelius has evinced more energy and "go aheadativeness" in building and driving steamboats, and other projects, than ever one single Dutchman possessed. It takes our American hot suns to clear off the vapors and fogs of the "Zuyder Zee," and wake up the phlegm of a descendant of old Holland.

name: Van Nest Abraham
biographical_note: An old Dutchman and self-made man; formerly a saddler, then engaged in the saddlery hardware business, in which, and by the rise of real estate, he has made his money.

name: Van Nostrand John
biographical_note: Respectable old Dutch family, from Jamaica, Long Island.

name: Van Schaick M.
biographical_note: Of a respectable Dutch family of Albany, and married the daughter of the rich Jno. Hone, and moved here. Has been a Senator.

name: Varian Isaac L.
biographical_note: Of the very oldest of the Dutch "Mynheers" who, some two centuries since, under, probably, Gov. Peter Minuit or Gov. Wm. Kieft, located with the Dyckmans, &c., as honest mechanics or farmers at Harlem, upon the outskirts of the island of Manhattan, Manahattas or Manhatoes, as this great city was variously called. There this colony, charmed with the muddy marshes of Spitendevil creek, so much like old Holland, remained and burroughed, and there they are to-day, till party strife has dragged some of them out into places of high prominence, like our late Mayor, Isaac; their harmless, industrious life being better political capital to any party than collegiate learning, as Isaac's success has proved, and his good judgment and plain democratic simplicity of manners weighing down all the disparagements of defective orthography and bad grammar.

name: Varnum Jos. B.
biographical_note: One of the ancient "noblesse" of Vermont.

name: Verplank Gulian C.
biographical_note: Son of the rich Judge V., of Fishhill, deceased. "Gu lem Verplanck," the ancestor, is one of the names found on the earliest Dutch records of New Amsterdam and though himself of plain origin, rose to consideration in municipal trusts after the Engish Conquest, 1646. Gulian C. V., late Senator, holds the most polished and classical pen in American literature, but wants the pathos and feeling of Irving, and the strength and energy of many other of our native writers. Elegant diction and philological and antiquarian studies were, however, the true field of his genius, and it was a fatal error when he aspired to political honors and senatorial dignities. The bitter feuds of party strife, and the low intrigues and associations of scurvy politicians, was not the life for him. To be compelled to compromise his pride and finer feelings, to favor those whom he imagined might promote his political advancement, led to treacherous combinations that forfeited his claims to the respect and confidence of both the great parties with whom he alternately acted as his caprice dictated. To the reigning and now prostra'e idols of mere party, he often thus sacrificed the considerations of private friendship and personal obligations, and thus tarnished, in the declining years of life, a name that should never have had its lustre dimned.

name: Wagstaff (Estate of David)
biographical_note: Had a fortunate father in the dry goods line.

name: Walker Joseph
biographical_note: English merchant.

name: Ward John
biographical_note: Brother of Samuel, deceased, and of Richard. Of an ancient and honored name in the annals of Rhode Island.

name: Ward Sam'l Jr.
biographical_note: Son of Samuel, deceased, (vide.) Married a daughter of the rich Wm. B. Astor, (vide;) and is of the banking house his father Samuel belonged to.

name: Wells James N.
biographical_note: James N. Wells rose from the humble vocation of a carpenter to be a rich man and alderman.

name: Wetmore William E.
biographical_note: A young man of Rhode Island of good birth, launched his bark with the true spirit of a New England boy on the wide waters of the ocean, and after buffeting the wave of every sea, from the Pacific and her summer gales to Asia's perfumed isles and the angry Atlantic, returns long before he has reached his prime, bringing home with him the huge fortune made by his own hands and by bold adventures, and reaching to near a million. Many is the lasso which wiley dowagers and old bel dames have thrown out to noose this Chinese Mandarin of the first water, but it won't do; he is not a Giraffe to be caught by such bait, for he sees the drift and measures the breadth of simpering twaddle driven to its last shuffle. He prefers to enjoy the luxury of his own palankin and punka solus "pour le present."

name: Weyman Abner
biographical_note: One of the richest "tailors" of our city. A worthy family.

name: White (Estate of) Charlotte and Amelia
biographical_note: Their father was of that high-toned circle of old English

gentlemen and merchants, who quondam dwelt in former times in that now much lampooned and inconsiderateey abused, Wall street, which, to believe certain infamous and obscene presses, is to day nothing better than a den of thieves and blacklegs, or robbers and murderers. The celebrated belles of this city in the revolution, when for seven years the martial trump and the plumed helmet of the red-coats flourished here, were decidedly these two charming sisters, the Miss Whites, but in the gay dance and the bewildering scenes of those spirit-stirring times, these fair damsels were too surrounded with England's noblest chivalry to know how to make up their minds, and thus lived on in the glorious independence of celibacy to these latter days-Miss Charlotte still surviving, They are some of the oldest of the English gentry of New York.

name: Whitney Stephen
biographical_note: Of the Whitneys of New England. As one obtained a pricelesss and goldless immortality, but no bread or provant for his family, by the invention of the cotton gin, which in a few short years drove the culture to hundreds of millions of pounds, so is Stephen in truth indebted to this same cotton and to this illustrious relative for the great fortune which he realized hand in hand "argent comptant," by the sudden rise of the article, in which the aforesaid Stephen, habitually cautious as he is, was luckily at the time a very heavy operator.

name: Whittemore Timothy
biographical_note: Another self-made man was his father, Samuel, by that curious and most ingenious of all ingenious Yankee inventions, the wool card maker, which by its complicated movements almost imitates the human hand, and human intelligence, self-propelling by steam. It is a rare instance of the inventions of genius giving return profits.

name: Williams Richard S.
biographical_note: By faithful, close application to the grocery business, in which he bears a most respectable name, has acquired a large property. He is of the family of one of the greatest landholders among the early purchasers and settlers who colonized the English villages on Long Island, viz: Robert Williams, nephew it is believed, of Roger, the founder of Providence.

name: Willis John R.
biographical_note: Hardware merchant, and of an ancient quaker family of Long Island.

name: Wilmerding Wm. E.
biographical_note: Of the auction house of Austin & Wilmerding, and formerly with Haggerty. He is, we believe, German in extraction.

name: Winthrop Henry R.
biographical_note: In expectancy this descendant of the first Governor of Massachusetts, may count on inheriting this amount by his marriage with Miss Hicks, a grand-daughter of the late Thomas Buchanan. The Winthrops have their family portraits for eight generations, as far back as the fifteenth century, when they left their rich possessions in England to found the city of Boston.

name: Woodruff Thos. T.
biographical_note: An architect, and while an alderman had several fat contracts of the corporation.

name: Wisner Gabriel
biographical_note: Grocer, and a worthy bachelor. His grandfather was a member of the Continental Congress from this state, and his father was killed at the Indian massacre at Minisink.

name: Wolfe Christopher
biographical_note: Hardware merchant, and of the old German families.

name: Wolfe J. D.
biographical_note: A hardware merchant; married one of the Lorillards by whom he has realized a very large property, with much more in expectation.

name: Wright (Estate of) Grove
biographical_note: This respected merchant, deceased, was probably a branch of the numerous family of Wrights of this city, who are of New England origin, having, it is believed, first setlled soon after the puritan forefathers at Plymouth.

name: Wright Jno. D.
biographical_note: We imagine this the rich son of Grove Wright above.

name: Wyckoff Henry
biographical_note: Old Dutch family.

name: Yates Henry
biographical_note: A lottery dealer &c. His brother was Gov. Joseph C Yates, one of the most ancient and respectable Dutch families that brought out their own goods and chattels, ships, household furniture, servants, blue jam tiles and olycoke and cruller irons, all from Old Amsterdam in Holland to Schenectady and those parts, long before the Livingstons and others of the "lesser empire" were dreamed of.

name: Yates Mrs. Joseph C.
biographical_note: Her husband of the Schenectady Yates family, made his wealth as a lottery dealer.

name: Young Henry
biographical_note: Of Long Island.
